title: Epic 1.1 — Snapshot Strategy
description:
Verify avt API surface, implement the terminal state snapshot mechanism using `Vt::dump()`/restore, and define the core types used by the keyframe index.

## Context

`avt::Vt` does **not** implement `Clone`. However, `Vt::dump()` serializes the full terminal state (both screen buffers, cursor, pen, modes, charsets, tab stops, scroll margins, saved cursor contexts) into an escape sequence string. Feeding this dump into a fresh `Vt` of the **same dimensions** reproduces the exact state. This is the chosen snapshot strategy.

### Key constraints

- `dump()` does NOT capture terminal dimensions — the caller must track and restore these.
- When primary buffer is active, alternate buffer **content** is not captured (only saved cursor context). This is correct: entering alternate mode always starts a fresh buffer.
- `Vt::new()` creates a non-resizable Vt. All Vts for playback must use `Vt::builder().size(w, h).resizable(true).scrollback_limit(0).build()`.
- `avt::Cursor` is not publicly importable — we define our own `CursorState` type.

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/snapshot.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs` and `Cargo.toml` (add `rgb = "0.8"` dependency)

## Tickets

- 1.1.1 — Snapshot types, capture, and restore
- 1.1.2 — Snapshot tests

title: 1.1.1 — Snapshot types, capture, and restore
notes:
## Codebase context

**Current state of files you will modify:**

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/lib.rs` contains only: `// speedrun-core library - Terminal session player core engine` (1 line, no modules).
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml` has deps `avt.workspace = true`, `serde.workspace = true`, `serde_json.workspace = true`. Dev-deps: `insta`, `tempfile`, `pretty_assertions` (all `.workspace = true`).
- Root `Cargo.toml` has `[workspace.dependencies]` with `avt = "0.11"`, `serde`, `serde_json`, etc. Add `rgb = "0.8"` in the same section.
- `snapshot.rs` does not exist yet — create it.

**Other epics may modify `lib.rs` concurrently.** Just add your `pub mod snapshot;` line and re-exports. Do not assume the file is empty or that you own it exclusively.

**Adding the `rgb` dependency — exact syntax:**
- Root `Cargo.toml`: add `rgb = "0.8"` under `[workspace.dependencies]`
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml`: add `rgb.workspace = true` under `[dependencies]`

## avt API quick reference

Key methods you will use (avt 0.11):

```
Vt::builder() -> Builder
Builder::size(cols: usize, rows: usize) -> &mut Builder
Builder::resizable(bool) -> &mut Builder
Builder::scrollback_limit(limit: usize) -> &mut Builder
Builder::build() -> Vt

vt.feed_str(s: &str) -> (Vec<usize>, bool)  // (dirty_lines, was_resized)
vt.dump() -> String          // serializes full state as escape sequences
vt.size() -> (usize, usize)  // (cols, rows) — NOTE: returns usize, not u16
vt.cursor() -> Cursor         // Cursor { pub col: usize, pub row: usize, pub visible: bool }
vt.view() -> &[Line]          // visible screen rows (exactly `rows` lines)
```

**Important**: `vt.size()` returns `(usize, usize)`. The snapshot stores `(u16, u16)`. You need an explicit cast: `width: size.0 as u16`.

## Verification commands

Run these before marking done:
```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.1.2 — Snapshot tests
description:
Comprehensive tests for snapshot capture/restore round-trips and cursor state extraction.

## Tests to implement

Add `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` to `snapshot.rs`:

1. **Basic round-trip**: Create Vt 10x4 via `create_vt(10, 4)`, feed `"hello\r\nworld"`, capture snapshot, restore to new Vt. Verify `view()` line text matches line-by-line (use `line.text()`). Verify `cursor()` col/row match between original and restored.

2. **Colors and attributes**: Feed escape sequences for bold (`\x1b[1m`), fg color (`\x1b[31m`), bg color (`\x1b[42m`), inverse (`\x1b[7m`). Write some text. Capture/restore. Verify attributes preserved by comparing `line.cells()` pen attributes (`is_bold()`, `foreground()`, etc.) on the restored Vt.

3. **Cursor state**: Test `CursorState::from_vt` across multiple scenarios in one test:
   - Default position (0, 0, visible) on a fresh Vt.
   - After feeding text to move cursor — verify col/row update.
   - Hide cursor (`\x1b[?25l`), capture/restore, verify `visible == false`.
   - Show cursor (`\x1b[?25h`), capture/restore, verify `visible == true`.

4. **Alternate buffer**: Enter alternate buffer (`\x1b[?1049h`), write text ("alt buffer content"), capture/restore, verify restored Vt shows the text via `view()`. Then in the same test or a follow-on assertion: exit alternate (`\x1b[?1049l`), write "primary content", capture/restore, verify primary buffer is correct. Add a comment explaining the asymmetry: alternate buffer content is intentionally not captured when primary is active — entering alternate always starts fresh.

5. **Resize**: Create Vt 80x24, feed resize escape sequence `\x1b[8;40;120t` (**note: xtwinops order is rows;cols**), write text. Capture snapshot. Assert `snapshot.width() == 120` and `snapshot.height() == 40`. Restore and verify `vt.size() == (120, 40)` and text content matches.

6. **Fresh Vt (empty recording edge case)**: Create Vt 80x24, capture immediately (no feed). Restore. Verify both are blank (all lines are empty/spaces) and cursors match (0, 0, visible).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo test -p speedrun-core -- snapshot` passes
- [ ] All 6 test cases are implemented and pass
- [ ] Resize test uses correct xtwinops parameter order (rows;cols, not cols;rows)
- [ ] Alternate buffer asymmetry is documented with a comment in the test
- [ ] Fresh/empty Vt round-trip is tested
notes:
## avt API reference for tests

You will need these avt APIs to write assertions:

```
// Line text extraction
line.text() -> String              // plain text of a line

// Cell iteration (for attribute verification)
line.cells() -> impl Iterator<Item = (char, Pen)>

// Pen attribute getters
pen.foreground() -> Option<Color>  // Color::Indexed(u8) or Color::RGB(rgb::RGB8)
pen.background() -> Option<Color>
pen.is_bold() -> bool
pen.is_faint() -> bool
pen.is_italic() -> bool
pen.is_underline() -> bool
pen.is_strikethrough() -> bool
pen.is_blink() -> bool
pen.is_inverse() -> bool

// Color types
avt::Color::Indexed(u8)
avt::Color::RGB(rgb::RGB8)    // rgb::RGB8 has pub fields r, g, b
```

**Comparing restored state**: The simplest approach is to compare line text (`line.text()`) for content and spot-check specific cells for attributes. Do NOT try to compare `Line` directly (it has private fields).

**The testdata_path helper** is mentioned for convention only — this ticket builds all Vts in-memory. Do not import testdata files.

## Verification commands

Run these before marking done:
```bash
cargo test -p speedrun-core -- snapshot
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

description:
## Summary

Implement the `?` help overlay: a centered panel listing all keybindings grouped by category, with toggle/dismiss behavior and playback state preservation.

## Context

No help overlay exists. The `ToggleHelp` action variant was added by the foundation ticket but is a no-op. Playback must pause while help is visible and resume (if it was playing) on dismiss. The `Esc` key currently maps to `Quit` — when help is visible, dispatch must intercept `Quit` and dismiss help instead.

## Dependency

Depends on the foundation ticket (3.0 — Action enum) being merged first.

## Scope

- Help overlay ratatui widget with grouped keybindings
- Toggle/dismiss behavior (?, Esc)
- Playback state preservation (pause on show, resume on dismiss)
- Esc context-dependent handling
- Terminal-too-small graceful degradation

## Tickets

1. 3.4.1 — Help overlay widget
2. 3.4.2 — Help overlay behavior

## Requirements covered

From REQUIREMENTS.md Epic 3.4 (lines 619-635).

title: 3.4.1 — Help overlay widget
description:
## Summary

Implement the help overlay as a centered ratatui widget displaying all keybindings grouped by category.

## Context

No help overlay exists. The widget should be a pure rendering component — behavior (toggle, pause/resume) is handled in ticket 3.4.2.

## Files

- `crates/speedrun/src/ui.rs` — add `HelpOverlay` widget (or a `help.rs` submodule re-exported from `ui.rs`)

## Tasks

### 1. Define keybinding data

Structure the keybindings into groups:

```rust
struct KeybindingGroup {
    title: &'static str,
    bindings: &'static [(&'static str, &'static str)],  // (key, description)
}

const KEYBINDING_GROUPS: &[KeybindingGroup] = &[
    KeybindingGroup {
        title: "Playback",
        bindings: &[
            ("Space", "Play / pause"),
            ("+ / =", "Speed up"),
            ("-", "Speed down"),
        ],
    },
    KeybindingGroup {
        title: "Navigation",
        bindings: &[
            ("← →", "Seek ±5s"),
            ("Shift+← →", "Seek ±30s"),
            (". ,", "Step forward / back"),
            ("] [", "Next / prev marker"),
            ("0-9", "Jump to 0%-90%"),
            ("Home / g", "Jump to start"),
            ("End / G", "Jump to end"),
        ],
    },
    KeybindingGroup {
        title: "Display",
        bindings: &[
            ("Tab", "Toggle controls"),
            ("?", "Toggle help"),
        ],
    },
    KeybindingGroup {
        title: "Application",
        bindings: &[
            ("q / Esc", "Quit"),
        ],
    },
];
```

### 2. Widget struct

```rust
pub struct HelpOverlay;
```

No state needed — the widget is purely presentational. It always renders the same content.

### 3. Layout calculation

Calculate the overlay dimensions:
- Width: widest line across all groups + padding + border (2 chars each side). Typically ~35-40 chars.
- Height: sum of all group titles + all bindings + spacing between groups + border (2 lines) + title line.
- Center horizontally and vertically within the render area.

If the terminal is too small to fit the overlay:
- If height doesn't fit: render as much as fits, clipping the bottom
- If width doesn't fit: render at full terminal width, content may clip
- If terminal is extremely small (< 10x5): render a simple "Terminal too small" message centered, or render nothing

### 4. Widget::render implementation

Use ratatui primitives:
- `Clear` widget to blank the overlay area (ensures content beneath doesn't show through)
- `Block` with `Borders::ALL` and title `" Keybindings "` (centered)
- Inside the block, render each group:
  - Group title in bold/highlighted style
  - Each binding as: `  {key:<12}  {description}` (left-aligned key column, then description)
  - Blank line between groups (except after the last)

Styling:
- Border: default or light style
- Group titles: bold
- Key column: distinct color (e.g., `Color::Yellow` or `Color::Cyan`)
- Description: default foreground
- Background: solid color to obscure content beneath (e.g., default terminal background or `Color::Black`)

### 5. Snapshot tests

Render `HelpOverlay` into a `Buffer` and snapshot:
- **Standard size** (60x25): full overlay visible, centered
- **Small terminal** (30x15): overlay fills most of the space, may clip
- **Minimum terminal** (15x5): graceful degradation

Use `insta::assert_snapshot!` for each.

### 6. Unit tests

- Overlay dimensions calculation: verify width and height for the known keybinding set
- Centering: verify the overlay `Rect` is centered for various terminal sizes

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `HelpOverlay` widget renders a centered bordered panel with title "Keybindings"
- [ ] Keybindings are grouped into four categories: Playback, Navigation, Display, Application
- [ ] Each group has a highlighted title followed by key-description pairs
- [ ] Key column is visually distinct (color or style) from descriptions
- [ ] Overlay is horizontally and vertically centered in the available area
- [ ] Terminal too small: degrades gracefully (clips or shows minimal message), no panic
- [ ] Snapshot tests cover standard, small, and minimum terminal sizes
- [ ] Widget renders on top of content (uses Clear or solid background)
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes

title: 3.3.1 — Speed cycling
description:
## Summary

Implement `SpeedUp` and `SpeedDown` action dispatch with a fixed speed set and boundary clamping.

## Context

The foundation ticket added `SpeedUp` and `SpeedDown` to the `Action` enum and mapped `+`/`=` and `-` keys. The dispatch arms are currently no-ops. `Player::set_speed(f64)` and `Player::speed()` already exist in the core library and accept arbitrary f64 values. This ticket adds the speed-step logic in the TUI layer.

## Files

- `crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` — implement SpeedUp/SpeedDown dispatch

## Tasks

### 1. Define speed steps

Define the fixed speed set as a constant:
```rust
const SPEED_STEPS: &[f64] = &[0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 4.0];
```

### 2. Implement speed cycling helper

`fn next_speed(current: f64, direction: i8) -> f64`
- `direction = 1` (up): find the smallest value in `SPEED_STEPS` that is strictly greater than `current`. If none, return the last (4.0). 
- `direction = -1` (down): find the largest value in `SPEED_STEPS` that is strictly less than `current`. If none, return the first (0.25).
- If `current` is not exactly on a step (e.g., set via `--speed 0.75`), snap to the nearest step in the requested direction.
- Use epsilon comparison (`(a - b).abs() < 1e-9`) for floating point equality when checking if current matches a step.

### 3. Wire dispatch

```rust
Action::SpeedUp => {
    let new_speed = next_speed(self.player.speed(), 1);
    self.player.set_speed(new_speed);
    self.show_controls = true;
    self.last_interaction = Instant::now();
}
Action::SpeedDown => {
    let new_speed = next_speed(self.player.speed(), -1);
    self.player.set_speed(new_speed);
    self.show_controls = true;
    self.last_interaction = Instant::now();
}
```

**Important — parallel epic coordination:** This epic runs in a separate worktree from the controls bar epic (3.1+3.2). The controls bar epic may replace `show_controls: bool` with a richer visibility state machine (e.g., `controls_force_show`, `controls_manually_hidden`). Since you are working in an isolated branch, just use the fields that exist in your worktree at the time. The integration ticket (3.5) will reconcile any differences. At minimum, set `self.show_controls = true` and `self.last_interaction = Instant::now()` — the integration step will adapt these to the final controls API.

### 4. Unit tests

- `next_speed(1.0, 1)` → `1.5`
- `next_speed(1.0, -1)` → `0.5`
- `next_speed(4.0, 1)` → `4.0` (clamped at max)
- `next_speed(0.25, -1)` → `0.25` (clamped at min)
- `next_speed(0.75, 1)` → `1.0` (snaps up from non-step value)
- `next_speed(0.75, -1)` → `0.5` (snaps down from non-step value)
- `next_speed(0.25, 1)` → `0.5` (from min going up)
- `next_speed(4.0, -1)` → `2.0` (from max going down)
- Full cycle up: 0.25 → 0.5 → 1.0 → 1.5 → 2.0 → 4.0 → 4.0
- Full cycle down: 4.0 → 2.0 → 1.5 → 1.0 → 0.5 → 0.25 → 0.25
- Integration: construct App, simulate SpeedUp action, verify `player.speed()` changed

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `SPEED_STEPS` constant defined with values `[0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 4.0]`
- [ ] `SpeedUp` action moves to next higher speed step; clamps at 4.0×
- [ ] `SpeedDown` action moves to next lower speed step; clamps at 0.25×
- [ ] Non-standard current speed (e.g., `--speed 0.75`) snaps to nearest step in the requested direction
- [ ] Speed changes trigger controls bar visibility (set `show_controls = true` or equivalent)
- [ ] `last_interaction` is updated on speed change
- [ ] Unit tests cover all step transitions, boundary clamping, and non-step snapping
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes

title: 0.2 — Tooling and pre-commit hooks
description:
Set up formatting, linting, and pre-commit hooks so that CI-equivalent checks run locally before every commit.

## Tasks

- Create `rustfmt.toml` at workspace root with default config (empty file — use rustfmt defaults).
- Add `[workspace.lints.clippy]` to root `Cargo.toml` with `all = "deny"`. Both crates inherit via `[lints] workspace = true`.
- Create a pre-commit hook script at `scripts/install-hooks.sh` that symlinks `.git/hooks/pre-commit` to a `scripts/pre-commit` script. The pre-commit script runs, in order:
  1. `cargo fmt --check` — reject unformatted code
  2. `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` — reject lint warnings
  3. `cargo test --workspace` — reject failing tests
  If any step fails, the commit is aborted with a clear message indicating which check failed.
- The install script should be idempotent (safe to run multiple times).
- Document in a comment at the top of `install-hooks.sh`: run `bash scripts/install-hooks.sh` after cloning.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `rustfmt.toml` exists at workspace root
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes on the scaffold
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes on the scaffold
- [ ] `scripts/pre-commit` exists and is executable
- [ ] `scripts/install-hooks.sh` exists and is executable
- [ ] After running `install-hooks.sh`, a commit with a fmt violation (e.g., extra spaces) is rejected by the hook
- [ ] After running `install-hooks.sh`, a commit with a clippy warning is rejected by the hook
- [ ] Running `install-hooks.sh` twice does not error or create duplicate hooks

title: 0.3 — Test data
description:
Create synthetic .cast files for testing. All files are hand-crafted, minimal, and human-readable. These are the fixtures that Phase 1 parser tests will validate against.

## Asciicast format reference

**V2 header:** First line is a JSON object. Required fields: `version` (int, must be 2), `width` (int), `height` (int). Optional: `timestamp` (unix epoch), `idle_time_limit` (float, seconds), `title` (string), `env` (object).
Example: `{"version": 2, "width": 80, "height": 24}`

**V2 events:** Subsequent lines are JSON arrays: `[timestamp, "event_type", "data"]`.
Timestamps are absolute seconds from start (float). Event types: `o` (output), `i` (input), `m` (marker with label as data), `r` (resize, data is `"COLSxROWS"`).
Example: `[0.5, "o", "hello\r\n"]`

**V3 header:** Same as V2 but `"version": 3`.
**V3 events:** Same array shape but timestamps are relative (interval since previous event, not absolute).

## Valid test files (testdata/)

- `minimal_v2.cast`: V2 header (80x24) + 3-5 output events with varying timestamps (e.g., 0.5s, 1.2s, 2.0s). Content: simple text like `$ hello\r\nworld\r\n`.
- `minimal_v3.cast`: V3 header (80x24) + 3-5 output events with relative timestamps (e.g., 0.5, 0.7, 0.8). Same kind of simple output.
- `empty.cast`: Valid V2 header, zero event lines.
- `with_markers.cast`: V2 header + mix of output and at least 2 marker (`m`) events with labels. E.g., `[3.0, "m", "chapter-1"]`, `[7.0, "m", "chapter-2"]`.
- `with_resize.cast`: V2 header (80x24) + output events + at least 1 resize (`r`) event. E.g., `[2.0, "r", "120x40"]`.
- `long_idle.cast`: V2 header with `"idle_time_limit": 2`. Events: output at 1.0s, then output at 35.0s (34-second gap), then output at 36.0s. This tests idle capping.
- `alternate_buffer.cast`: V2 header + output events that switch to alternate screen buffer and back. Use these exact sequences:
  - Enter alt buffer: `\u001b[?1049h`
  - Write something in alt buffer
  - Exit alt buffer: `\u001b[?1049l`
- `real_session.cast`: A realistic recording, 30+ seconds long. Hand-craft ~20 output events over 30s simulating a shell session (commands, output, prompts with realistic timing gaps).

## Invalid test files (testdata/invalid/)

- `empty_file.cast`: Exactly zero bytes.
- `no_header.cast`: Event lines only, no JSON header on line 1. E.g., `[0.5, "o", "hello"]` as first line.
- `bad_json.cast`: Invalid JSON on line 1. E.g., `{"version": 2, "width": 80, "height": 24` (missing closing brace).
- `bad_version.cast`: Valid JSON header but `"version": 99`.
- `missing_fields.cast`: Valid JSON header but missing `width` and `height`. E.g., `{"version": 2}`.
- `bad_event.cast`: Valid header followed by malformed events: `[0.5, "o"]` (missing data), `not json at all`, `[null, "o", "x"]` (null timestamp).
- `binary_garbage.cast`: 64 bytes of random binary content (non-UTF8).
- `truncated.cast`: Valid header + one valid event + one incomplete line (e.g., `[1.5, "o", "hel`).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All valid .cast files exist in `testdata/`
- [ ] All invalid .cast files exist in `testdata/invalid/`
- [ ] Each valid file's first line is parseable JSON with the correct `version` field
- [ ] `long_idle.cast` header has `"idle_time_limit": 2`
- [ ] `with_markers.cast` contains at least 2 lines with `"m"` event type
- [ ] `with_resize.cast` contains at least 1 line with `"r"` event type
- [ ] `alternate_buffer.cast` contains the `1049h` and `1049l` escape sequences
- [ ] `empty_file.cast` is exactly 0 bytes
- [ ] `binary_garbage.cast` is not valid UTF-8
- [ ] File count: 8 valid + 8 invalid = 16 total .cast files

title: 0.4 — Populate AGENTS.md
description:
Populate the root AGENTS.md file with project-specific agent onboarding content. This is the file `pit init` created — it already contains pit orchestration instructions at the bottom. This ticket adds the project-specific sections above them.

This is a Human + Agent collaborative task. Do NOT autoclose.

## Sections to include

1. **Project overview** — speedrun is a terminal session player for asciicast (.cast) files with instant seeking and scrubbing. Two-crate Cargo workspace: `speedrun-core` (library, no UI) and `speedrun` (TUI binary).
2. **Build and test commands** — `cargo build --workspace`, `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`. Single crate: `cargo test -p speedrun-core`.
3. **Project structure** — Brief map of `crates/speedrun-core/src/` (lib.rs, parser.rs, timemap.rs, index.rs, player.rs, snapshot.rs) and `crates/speedrun/src/` (main.rs, app.rs, ui.rs, input.rs). What each file is responsible for.
4. **Issue tracking** — beads workflow: `bd ready`, `bd show <id>`, `bd update <id> --status in_progress`, `bd close <id>`, `bd sync`.
5. **Coding conventions** — Rust edition 2024, clippy deny warnings, rustfmt defaults. No `unwrap()` in library code (use typed errors). `insta` for snapshot tests. Workspace-level dependency declarations.
6. **Key dependencies** — `avt` (terminal emulation), `ratatui`/`crossterm` (TUI), `clap` (CLI), `serde`/`serde_json` (asciicast parsing).

## Also

## Acceptance criteria

title: 2.3.1 — Wire TerminalView into app and fix loop ordering
description:
Replace the placeholder Paragraph render with the live TerminalView widget, add viewport scroll tracking, fix the event loop ordering, add conditional re-render, and make crossterm read errors non-fatal.

## File

`crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` only.

## Tasks

1. **Add ViewportState to App.** Import `ViewportState` from `crate::ui`. Add `viewport: ViewportState` field to the `App` struct. Initialize as `ViewportState::default()` in `App::new()`.

2. **Change render() to &mut self.** The method needs mutable access to update viewport scroll offsets before rendering.

3. **Replace placeholder with TerminalView.** Inside `render()`:
   - Call `self.viewport.follow_cursor()` with the cursor position from `self.player.cursor()`, the recording size from `self.player.size()`, and the frame area dimensions.
   - Construct `TerminalView::new(self.player.screen(), self.player.cursor(), self.player.size()).with_scroll(self.viewport.scroll_x, self.viewport.scroll_y)`.
   - Dispatch via `frame.render_widget(view, area)`.

4. **Fix loop ordering.** Current order is render → poll → tick → quit. Correct order: poll → input → tick → render → quit. Move the `terminal.draw()` call to after the tick block. Ensure first iteration still renders (the initial frame must appear before the first poll wait).

5. **Add conditional re-render.** Track a `needs_redraw: bool`. Set to `true`:
   - On first iteration (before the loop or via initialization)
   - When `handle_key()` is called (any keypress)
   - When `Event::Resize` is received
   - When `tick()` returns `true`
   Only call `terminal.draw()` when `needs_redraw` is true. Reset after drawing.

6. **Make crossterm read errors non-fatal.** Change the `event::read()?` call so that errors are logged to stderr and the loop continues instead of propagating the error and exiting. Use `eprintln!` for logging.

7. **Extract compute_timeout() helper.** Extract the timeout calculation into `fn compute_timeout(&self) -> Duration` that returns `self.player.time_to_next_event().unwrap_or(Duration::from_millis(100))`. This makes it testable from 2.3.3.

8. **Remove dead code.** Delete the placeholder `Paragraph` and its unused imports: `Block`, `Borders`, `Paragraph` from the `ratatui::widgets` import.

## Important: ratatui 0.26 API

This project uses ratatui 0.26.3. In this version, `Frame` has `fn size(&self) -> Rect`, NOT `fn area()`. The `area()` method does not exist in 0.26. The current code already uses `frame.size()` correctly — do NOT change it to `frame.area()`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `App` has a `viewport: ViewportState` field initialized to default
- [ ] `render()` is `&mut self`, calls `viewport.follow_cursor()`, then renders `TerminalView` with live player state and scroll offsets
- [ ] Loop order is: poll → input → tick → conditional render → quit check
- [ ] `terminal.draw()` is skipped when nothing changed (no input, no resize, tick() returned false)
- [ ] First frame renders immediately (before first poll wait)
- [ ] `event::read()` errors are logged to stderr and the loop continues
- [ ] `compute_timeout()` is a separate `pub` method returning `Duration`
- [ ] Placeholder Paragraph and Block/Borders/Paragraph imports are removed
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes

title: 2.3.2 — Wire --keyframe-interval through the full stack
description:
The --keyframe-interval CLI arg is parsed in main.rs but silently dropped — LoadOptions has no keyframe_interval field, so the value never reaches KeyframeIndex::build(). This ticket wires the option through the full chain.

## Files

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` — add field to LoadOptions, pass through in load_with()
- `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` — build() takes interval parameter
- `crates/speedrun/src/main.rs` — pass args.keyframe_interval into LoadOptions

## Tasks

1. **Add keyframe_interval to LoadOptions** in `player.rs`. Add `pub keyframe_interval: f64` to `LoadOptions`. Replace the current `#[derive(Default)]` with a manual `impl Default for LoadOptions` that sets `idle_limit: None` and `keyframe_interval: KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` (the 5.0 constant from `index.rs`). This is necessary because `f64` derives to `0.0`, not `5.0`.

2. **Change KeyframeIndex::build() signature** in `index.rs`. Add `interval: f64` parameter. Replace the internal use of the `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` constant with this parameter. The constant itself can remain as the canonical default value (referenced by LoadOptions::default()) but must not be hardcoded inside build().

3. **Pass through in Player::load_with()** in `player.rs`. Change the `KeyframeIndex::build()` call to pass `opts.keyframe_interval` as the interval argument.

4. **Wire in main.rs.** Change the LoadOptions construction to:
   ```rust
   let opts = speedrun_core::LoadOptions {
       idle_limit: args.idle_limit,
       keyframe_interval: args.keyframe_interval,
   };
   ```

## Important: Default impl

`LoadOptions` currently uses `#[derive(Default)]`. Adding `keyframe_interval: f64` breaks this because `f64::default()` is `0.0`, not `5.0`. You MUST replace `#[derive(Default)]` with a manual impl:

```rust
impl Default for LoadOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            idle_limit: None,
            keyframe_interval: KEYFRAME_INTERVAL,
        }
    }
}
```

Import `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` from `crate::index::KEYFRAME_INTERVAL`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `LoadOptions` has `pub keyframe_interval: f64` field
- [ ] `LoadOptions` has a manual `impl Default` that sets `keyframe_interval` to `5.0` (via `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL`)
- [ ] `#[derive(Default)]` is removed from `LoadOptions`
- [ ] `KeyframeIndex::build()` takes `interval: f64` as a parameter, uses it instead of the hardcoded constant
- [ ] `Player::load_with()` passes `opts.keyframe_interval` to `KeyframeIndex::build()`
- [ ] `main.rs` passes `args.keyframe_interval` into `LoadOptions`
- [ ] All existing keyframe index tests pass unchanged (they use default interval via Player::load which uses LoadOptions::default)
- [ ] `cargo build --workspace` passes with zero warnings

- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes

title: 2.3.3 — App render and timeout integration tests
description:
Add integration tests that exercise the full App::render() path (ViewportState + TerminalView dispatch) and the compute_timeout() helper, using ratatui TestBackend so no real TTY is needed.

## Files

New tests in `crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` (as a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block) or `crates/speedrun/tests/` integration test file.

## Context

- Ticket 2.3.1 added `App::render()` using TerminalView and `App::compute_timeout()` as a pub method.
- `ui.rs` already has snapshot tests for TerminalView in isolation. These tests are different — they test the full App render path (viewport update + widget dispatch) and the timeout logic.
- `minimal_v2.cast` at end-of-recording has known content: row 0 = "\$ hello", row 1 = "world", row 2 = "\$ exit", row 3 = "logout".

## Tasks

1. **Render path test.** Write a test that:
   - Loads `testdata/minimal_v2.cast` via `Player::load()`
   - Seeks to `player.duration()` (known screen state)
   - Constructs `App::new(player, true)`
   - Creates a `Terminal<TestBackend>` sized to the recording dimensions (80x24)
   - Calls `terminal.draw(|f| app.render(f))`
   - Reads the buffer and asserts row 0 starting from column 0 contains the characters `$ hello`
   
   This verifies the full chain: player screen state → viewport follow_cursor → TerminalView construction → buffer write.

2. **Timeout calculation tests.** Write tests for `App::compute_timeout()`:
   - **Paused state:** Load a file, don't call `play()`. Assert `compute_timeout()` returns `Duration::from_millis(100)` (the fallback, since `time_to_next_event()` returns None when paused).
   - **Playing state:** Load a file, call `play()`. Assert `compute_timeout()` returns the value from `player.time_to_next_event()` (which for minimal_v2.cast at t=0 playing at 1x is ~500ms).
   - **At end:** Load a file, play, tick past the end. Player auto-pauses. Assert `compute_timeout()` returns `Duration::from_millis(100)`.

## TestBackend buffer access pattern (ratatui 0.26)

In ratatui 0.26, read the rendered buffer like this:

```rust
use ratatui::backend::TestBackend;
use ratatui::Terminal;

let backend = TestBackend::new(80, 24);
let mut terminal = Terminal::new(backend).unwrap();
terminal.draw(|f| app.render(f)).unwrap();

// Read cells from the buffer:
let buf = terminal.backend().buffer();
let cell_text: String = (0..7).map(|x| buf.get(x, 0).symbol()).collect();
assert_eq!(cell_text, "$ hello");
```

`terminal.backend()` returns `&TestBackend`, and `TestBackend` has a `buffer()` method returning `&Buffer`. Use `buf.get(x, y).symbol()` to read individual cell contents.

## Notes

- Use `ratatui::backend::TestBackend` — not a real TTY.
- The test file path: use `env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")` to build the path (consistent with how speedrun-core tests locate test data), e.g. `format!("{}/../../testdata/minimal_v2.cast", env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR"))`.
- `App::render()` takes `&mut self` (changed in 2.3.1), so the app variable must be mutable.
- `terminal.draw()` takes `FnOnce(&mut Frame)`. Use `terminal.draw(|f| app.render(f))` inside the test.
- This project uses ratatui 0.26.3. `Frame` has `fn size()`, NOT `fn area()`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Render test uses `ratatui::backend::TestBackend` and `Terminal`, not a real TTY
- [ ] Render test loads `minimal_v2.cast`, seeks to duration, constructs App, calls render via terminal.draw()
- [ ] Render test asserts buffer row 0 starts with `$ hello`
- [ ] Timeout test covers three states: paused (100ms), playing (event-driven), ended (100ms)
- [ ] Tests are in the speedrun crate (not speedrun-core) — they test App, not Player
- [ ] Tests are distinct from existing ui.rs widget snapshot tests
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes

title: Epic 1.3 — Time Mapper
description:
Apply idle time limits to produce effective timestamps for each event. Computed once at load time.

## Context

Recordings may contain long idle gaps (e.g., 30+ seconds of inactivity). The idle limit (from CLI flag, header, or none) caps these gaps to produce a compressed "effective" timeline. All public API surfaces (progress bar, seek, duration) use effective time.

### Key design decisions

- **Decoupled from parser types**: `TimeMap::build()` takes a `&[f64]` of raw timestamps, not `&[Event]`. The caller extracts timestamps.
- **Idle limit as `Option<f64>`**: `None` = no capping (effective = raw). No enum wrapper.
- **First event time preserved**: the gap between t=0 and the first event is NOT capped. Only inter-event gaps are subject to idle limiting.
- **Binary search for seeking**: `event_index_at(time)` returns `Option<usize>` — the last event at or before the given effective time. Returns `None` for empty maps or times before the first event.

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/timemap.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.3.1 — TimeMap implementation
- 1.3.2 — TimeMap tests

title: 1.3.1 — TimeMap implementation
description:
Implement the time mapper that converts raw event timestamps to effective (idle-capped) timestamps.

## Cross-cutting conventions

- **Idle limit as `Option<f64>`**: `None` = no capping. No enum wrapper.
- **Decoupled from parser**: `TimeMap::build()` takes `&[f64]` (raw timestamps), NOT `&[Event]`. The caller extracts timestamps. This keeps the module independent for parallel development.
- **Derives**: `TimeMap`: `Debug`. `TimeMapError`: `Debug`.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/timemap.rs` with:

   ```rust
   /// Mapping from raw timestamps to effective (idle-capped) timestamps.
   /// Computed once at load time. One entry per event, parallel to the events vec.
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub struct TimeMap {
       effective_times: Vec<f64>,
       duration: f64,
   }

   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum TimeMapError {
       /// Idle limit must be positive.
       InvalidIdleLimit(f64),
   }
   ```
   Implement `std::fmt::Display` and `std::error::Error` for `TimeMapError`.

2. **Construction**:
   ```rust
   impl TimeMap {
       /// Build a time map from raw event timestamps.
       ///
       /// `idle_limit`: if `Some(limit)`, inter-event gaps exceeding `limit`
       /// seconds are capped to `limit`. If `None`, effective = raw.
       pub fn build(raw_times: &[f64], idle_limit: Option<f64>) -> Result<Self, TimeMapError>
   }
   ```

3. **Algorithm**:
   - If `idle_limit` is `Some(limit)` and `limit <= 0.0` → return `Err(InvalidIdleLimit(limit))`.
   - If `raw_times` is empty → return `Ok(TimeMap { effective_times: vec![], duration: 0.0 })`.
   - `effective_times[0] = raw_times[0]`. The gap between t=0 and the first event is NOT capped — it is the recording's natural start offset.
   - For `i` in `1..len`:
     - `gap = raw_times[i] - raw_times[i-1]`
     - `capped_gap = match idle_limit { Some(limit) => gap.min(limit), None => gap }`
     - `effective_times[i] = effective_times[i-1] + capped_gap`
   - `duration = *effective_times.last().unwrap_or(&0.0)`

4. **Accessors**:
   ```rust
   /// Effective timestamp for event at index. None if out of bounds.
   pub fn effective_time(&self, index: usize) -> Option<f64>

   /// Total effective duration (effective time of last event, or 0.0 if empty).
   pub fn duration(&self) -> f64

   /// Number of entries (same as number of events).
   pub fn len(&self) -> usize
   pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool

   /// Find the last event at or before `time` in effective time.
   /// Returns None if the time map is empty or time is before the first event.
   pub fn event_index_at(&self, time: f64) -> Option<usize>
   ```

5. **`event_index_at` implementation**: Use `partition_point` for binary search on `effective_times`. Find the number of entries where `effective_times[i] <= time`, then return `that_count - 1` (the last entry at-or-before). If the count is 0 (time is before all entries), return `None`. If the vec is empty, return `None`.

   Note on f64 precision: the values produced by this algorithm are simple additions/subtractions of small numbers. No epsilon tolerance is needed — exact `<=` comparison is correct for the values we produce.

6. Add `pub mod timemap;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export: `pub use timemap::{TimeMap, TimeMapError};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo build -p speedrun-core` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `TimeMap::build` handles: no limit, with limit, empty input, invalid limit (<=0)
- [ ] First event time is preserved (not capped)
- [ ] Inter-event gaps exceeding the limit are capped; gaps at or below limit are untouched
- [ ] `event_index_at` returns `None` for empty maps and times before first event
- [ ] `event_index_at` returns correct index for exact matches, between-events, and past-end
- [ ] `TimeMapError` implements `Display` and `Error`
- [ ] Types are exported from `lib.rs`
notes:
## Codebase context

**Current state of files you will modify:**

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/lib.rs` contains only: `// speedrun-core library - Terminal session player core engine` (1 line, no modules).
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml` already has all needed deps. No new dependencies required for this module.
- `timemap.rs` does not exist yet — create it.

**Other epics may modify `lib.rs` concurrently** (snapshot.rs and parser.rs are being built in parallel). Just add your `pub mod timemap;` line and re-exports. Do not assume the file is empty or that you own it exclusively.

**This module has no dependency on the parser module.** `TimeMap::build()` takes `&[f64]` (raw timestamps), not `&[Event]`. This is deliberate — it keeps the epics independent for parallel development.


title: 1.3.2 — TimeMap tests
description:
Tests for the time mapper. Tests use raw timestamp slices directly — no dependency on the parser module.

## Tests to implement

Add `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` to `timemap.rs`:

### Core behavior

1. **Idle limit capping**: Raw times `[1.0, 1.1, 35.0, 35.1, 36.0]`. Test three scenarios in one test (or three closely related tests):
   - `idle_limit: Some(2.0)` → effective `[1.0, 1.1, 3.1, 3.2, 4.1]`, duration `4.1`
   - `idle_limit: Some(1.0)` → effective `[1.0, 1.1, 2.1, 2.2, 3.1]`, duration `3.1`
   - `idle_limit: None` → effective equals raw, duration `36.0`

2. **Empty event list**: `raw_times: &[]`, `idle_limit: Some(2.0)`. Duration `0.0`, len `0`, is_empty `true`.

3. **Single event**: Raw `[5.0]`, `idle_limit: Some(2.0)`. Effective `[5.0]`, duration `5.0`.

4. **Multiple consecutive idle gaps**: Raw `[0.0, 1.0, 20.0, 21.0, 40.0]`, limit `Some(2.0)`. Two gaps capped. Expected effective: `[0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 4.0, 6.0]`, duration `6.0`.

5. **First event at t>0**: Raw `[5.0, 6.0, 7.0]`, limit `Some(2.0)`. First event time 5.0 is preserved (not capped). Expected: `[5.0, 6.0, 7.0]`, duration `7.0`.

### Error cases

6. **Invalid idle limits**: `Some(0.0)` and `Some(-1.0)` both → `TimeMapError::InvalidIdleLimit`. Can be one test with two assertions.

### `event_index_at` tests

7. **Binary search correctness**: Build a TimeMap from test 1 (effective times `[1.0, 1.1, 3.1, 3.2, 4.1]`). Verify:
   - `event_index_at(0.5)` → `None` (before first)
   - `event_index_at(1.0)` → `Some(0)` (exact first)
   - `event_index_at(1.05)` → `Some(0)` (between)
   - `event_index_at(2.0)` → `Some(1)` (between)
   - `event_index_at(4.1)` → `Some(4)` (exact last)
   - `event_index_at(100.0)` → `Some(4)` (past end)
   - Empty map: `event_index_at(0.0)` → `None`
   - Single-event map (`[5.0]`): `event_index_at(4.0)` → `None`, `event_index_at(5.0)` → `Some(0)`

### Insta snapshot

8. Snapshot the effective times for the long_idle scenario (test 1, limit 2.0). Use `insta::assert_debug_snapshot!`.

### Edge case: `--at 0` vs omitting `--at`

- `speedrun export svg f.cast -o out.svg` → animated (no `--at`)

title: Add seamless infinite looping to animated SVG export
description:
The animated SVG currently plays once and stops (`animation-fill-mode: forwards`). Change to infinite looping with no visual gap at the iteration boundary.

## Current state

- Frame 0 (svg.rs:477): `animation: a0 Xs step-end forwards`
- Other frames (svg.rs:487): `visibility:hidden; animation: ai Xs step-end forwards`
- Last frame keyframe (svg.rs:491-494): only has a start point, no end — relies on `forwards` to stay visible
- Single-frame recordings: handled by degenerate-case logic in `build_frames` (svg.rs:574)

## Changes

### CSS generation (svg.rs, lines 466-501)

1. Replace `forwards` with `infinite` in ALL animation shorthand declarations:
   - Line 477 (frame 0): `step-end forwards` → `step-end infinite`
   - Line 487 (other frames): `step-end forwards` → `step-end infinite`

2. Add explicit `100%{visibility:hidden}` to the LAST frame's keyframe. Currently:
   ```css
   @keyframes aN{start_pct%{visibility:visible}}
   ```
   Change to:
   ```css
   @keyframes aN{start_pct%{visibility:visible}100%{visibility:hidden}}
   ```
   Without this, browser behavior at the iteration reset is undefined for the last frame. The explicit hide at 100% guarantees that when the animation wraps to 0%, only frame 0 is visible — no double-visibility flash.

3. Frame 0 already has `0%{visibility:visible}` which correctly re-triggers at each iteration start.

### Single-frame edge case

4. A single-frame recording with `infinite` iteration would constantly "animate" a single visibility toggle to no effect. When `build_frames` returns exactly one frame, skip the `<style>` block entirely — render the frame's `<g>` directly as visible (no animation, no id attribute needed). This avoids browser rendering overhead and produces cleaner SVG.

### Why this produces no gaps

With `step-end` and `infinite` iteration count:
- At the 100% → 0% boundary: last frame becomes hidden (explicit keyframe), frame 0 becomes visible (its `0%{visibility:visible}` keyframe)
- The transition is instantaneous — every percentage of the animation has exactly one visible `<g>` group
- Visual effect: recording plays → jumps to start → replays continuously

- Single-frame recordings produce SVG with no `<style>` block (static rendering, no animation)
- Multi-frame recordings loop seamlessly when opened in a browser (manual verification)
- Test `test_animated_fill_mode_forwards` is replaced: renamed to `test_animated_loops_infinitely`, asserts `infinite` present and `forwards` absent

- New test: `test_animated_single_frame_no_style_block` verifies single-frame SVG has no `<style>`
- `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- `cargo test --workspace` passes

title: 9.1 — Export crate scaffold and color palette
description:
Create the `speedrun-export` crate and implement the shared color palette infrastructure that all export formats depend on.

## Files to Create

- `crates/speedrun-export/Cargo.toml` — new crate manifest (edition = "2024")
- `crates/speedrun-export/src/lib.rs` — crate root with `pub mod palette;`
- `crates/speedrun-export/src/palette.rs` — `Palette` struct, color resolution, `ExportOptions`
- `Cargo.toml` (workspace root) — add `"crates/speedrun-export"` to `[workspace] members`

## Workspace Wiring

Add `crates/speedrun-export` to the workspace members in the root `Cargo.toml`. The new crate's `Cargo.toml` should specify:
- `edition = "2024"`
- Dependencies: `speedrun-core` (path = "../speedrun-core"), `avt` (same version as other crates), `rgb` (same version as other crates)
- Follow the same `[lints]` configuration as the other crates (deny clippy warnings)

Also add `speedrun-export` as an **optional dependency** of the `speedrun` TUI crate (`crates/speedrun/Cargo.toml`) behind a default-on feature flag `export`. This enables the `speedrun export` CLI subcommand. The feature wiring can be done now (scaffold) even if the subcommand isn't implemented until 9.2.

## Color Palette Resolver

Implement a `Palette` struct that resolves `avt::Color` variants to concrete `rgb::RGB8` values:
- `Color::Indexed(0-7)` -> standard xterm-256 colors
- `Color::Indexed(8-15)` -> bright xterm-256 colors
- `Color::Indexed(16-231)` -> 6x6x6 color cube (formula: r = (n-16)/36, g = ((n-16)%36)/6, b = (n-16)%6; value = if component == 0 { 0 } else { 55 + 40 * component })
- `Color::Indexed(232-255)` -> grayscale ramp (value = 8 + 10 * (n - 232))
- `Color::RGB(r, g, b)` -> passthrough as `rgb::RGB8`
- `None` (default fg/bg) -> configurable, defaults to `rgb::RGB8::new(208, 208, 208)` (fg) and `rgb::RGB8::new(30, 30, 30)` (bg)

## Bold + Standard Color Brightening

When the `Pen` has `is_bold() == true` and foreground is `Indexed(0-7)`, resolve to the bright variant `Indexed(n + 8)` before RGB lookup. This matches common terminal behavior. Provide a flag to disable this (`bold_brightens: bool` on `ExportOptions`).

## Inverse Attribute Handling

Provide a method `resolve_cell_colors(pen: &avt::Pen) -> (rgb::RGB8, rgb::RGB8)` that returns (foreground_rgb, background_rgb) with inverse handling applied: if `pen.is_inverse()`, swap fg and bg AFTER color resolution.

## ExportOptions Base Struct

```rust
pub struct ExportOptions {
    pub default_fg: rgb::RGB8,
    pub default_bg: rgb::RGB8,
    pub bold_brightens: bool,  // default: true
}

impl Default for ExportOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            default_fg: rgb::RGB8::new(208, 208, 208),
            default_bg: rgb::RGB8::new(30, 30, 30),
            bold_brightens: true,
        }
    }
}
```

## Tests

All tests go in `crates/speedrun-export/src/palette.rs` under `#[cfg(test)] mod tests`.

### Required test cases (minimum 14 tests):

#### Standard color table tests

1. **`test_indexed_color_0_black`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(0))` returns `RGB8::new(0, 0, 0)` (xterm black)
2. **`test_indexed_color_1_red`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(1))` returns `RGB8::new(205, 0, 0)` (xterm standard red, verify against xterm-256color chart)
3. **`test_indexed_color_9_bright_red`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(9))` returns the xterm bright red value
4. **`test_indexed_color_15_bright_white`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(15))` returns xterm bright white

#### Color cube tests

5. **`test_color_cube_index_16`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(16))` returns `RGB8::new(0, 0, 0)` (cube origin: all components 0)
6. **`test_color_cube_index_196`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(196))` returns the correct value. Calculate: `n=196`, `r=(196-16)/36=5`, `g=((196-16)%36)/6=0`, `b=(196-16)%6=0`. So `r=255, g=0, b=0` → `RGB8::new(255, 0, 0)`.
7. **`test_color_cube_index_231`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(231))` returns `RGB8::new(255, 255, 255)` (cube endpoint: all components 5 → 255)

#### Grayscale ramp tests

8. **`test_grayscale_index_232`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(232))` returns `RGB8::new(8, 8, 8)` (darkest gray: `8 + 10 * 0`)
9. **`test_grayscale_index_255`** — `resolve(Color::Indexed(255))` returns `RGB8::new(238, 238, 238)` (lightest gray: `8 + 10 * 23`)

#### Edge indexed value tests

10. **`test_indexed_out_of_range`** — Test what happens if `Color::Indexed(256)` is somehow passed (if avt allows it). Should not panic — either clamp or return a default. If avt constrains the range at the type level, skip this test and note in a comment why.

#### RGB passthrough test

11. **`test_rgb_passthrough`** — `resolve(Color::RGB(42, 128, 255))` returns `RGB8::new(42, 128, 255)` unchanged

#### Bold brightening tests

12. **`test_bold_brightens_standard_color`** — Create a `Pen` with `is_bold() == true` and foreground `Indexed(1)`. Call `resolve_cell_colors`. Verify foreground is resolved as `Indexed(9)` (bright red, not standard red).
13. **`test_bold_brightens_disabled`** — Same setup but with `ExportOptions { bold_brightens: false, .. }`. Verify foreground resolves to `Indexed(1)` (standard red, not brightened).
14. **`test_bold_does_not_brighten_high_index`** — `Pen` with `is_bold() == true` and foreground `Indexed(100)`. Verify the color is NOT shifted (bold brightening only applies to indices 0-7).

#### Inverse handling tests

15. **`test_inverse_swaps_fg_bg`** — Create a `Pen` with `is_inverse() == true`, foreground `Indexed(1)` (red), background `Indexed(4)` (blue). Call `resolve_cell_colors`. Verify foreground is blue, background is red (swapped).
16. **`test_inverse_with_defaults`** — `Pen` with `is_inverse() == true`, no explicit fg/bg (both None/default). Verify foreground becomes `default_bg` and background becomes `default_fg`.

#### Default color tests

17. **`test_default_fg_bg_configurable`** — Create `ExportOptions` with custom `default_fg: RGB8::new(0, 255, 0)`. Resolve a `None` foreground. Verify it returns `RGB8::new(0, 255, 0)`, not the hardcoded default.

### Note on avt::Pen construction in tests

The `avt::Pen` type may not have public constructors for all fields. Check how existing code creates `Pen` values. If `Pen` is opaque, create a small `avt::Vt` instance and feed it escape sequences to produce desired pen attributes:

```rust
fn pen_with_fg(color: avt::Color) -> avt::Pen {
    let mut vt = avt::Vt::new(1, 1);
    // Feed escape sequence to set foreground color
    // e.g., "\x1b[31m" for red, "\x1b[38;5;{n}m" for indexed
    vt.feed_str(&format!("\x1b[38;5;{}m ", color_to_index(color)));
    let line = &vt.view()[0];
    line.cells().next().unwrap().1.clone()
}
```

Investigate `avt::Pen`'s API before writing tests. If it has `Pen::default()` and builder methods, use those. If not, use the VT escape sequence approach above.

## Verification

```bash
cargo build --workspace
cargo test --workspace
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- `crates/speedrun-export/` exists and `cargo build --workspace` succeeds
- `cargo test --workspace` passes (including new tests in the export crate)
- `Palette::resolve(Color::Indexed(1))` returns xterm standard red RGB value
- `Palette::resolve(Color::Indexed(196))` returns the correct 6x6x6 cube value
- `Palette::resolve(Color::RGB(r, g, b))` returns `(r, g, b)` unchanged
- `resolve_cell_colors` with `is_inverse() == true` swaps foreground and background
- Bold + `Indexed(1)` resolves to `Indexed(9)` (bright red) when `bold_brightens` is true
- Bold + `Indexed(100)` does NOT shift the index (only 0-7 are brightened)
- Default fg/bg are configurable via `ExportOptions`
- Grayscale ramp indices 232-255 produce correct values
- Workspace root `Cargo.toml` lists `speedrun-export` as a member
- `speedrun-export/Cargo.toml` uses edition 2024
- At least 14 unit tests covering standard colors, cube, grayscale, bold brightening, inverse, and defaults

title: 9.2 — Static SVG export
description:
Render terminal state at a given point in time to a standalone SVG file. Includes CLI wiring.

## Files to Create/Modify

- `crates/speedrun-export/src/svg.rs` — SVG rendering logic
- `crates/speedrun-export/src/lib.rs` — add `pub mod svg;`
- `crates/speedrun/src/main.rs` — add `export svg` clap subcommand

## Public API

```rust
/// Render the current screen state as a static SVG document.
pub fn export_svg(
    player: &mut Player,
    options: &SvgOptions,
    writer: &mut dyn std::io::Write,
) -> Result<(), ExportError>;

pub struct SvgOptions {
    pub at_time: f64,         // effective time to capture (default: 0.0)
    pub font_size: f64,       // default: 14.0
    pub font_family: String,  // default: the font stack below
    pub show_cursor: bool,    // default: true
    pub export: ExportOptions, // palette config from 9.1
}
```

The function seeks to `at_time`, reads the screen, and writes SVG XML to `writer`.

## Rendering Approach

Use `line.segments()` (groups consecutive cells with the same `Pen`) to produce one `<text>` element per styled run. This is more efficient than per-cell rendering.

## SVG Structure

```xml
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 {width} {height}" font-family="{font_family}" font-size="{font_size}">
  <rect width="100%" height="100%" fill="{default_bg}"/>
  <!-- Per-line background rects for non-default bg segments -->
  <rect x="..." y="..." width="..." height="..." fill="{bg}"/>
  <!-- Per-segment text elements -->
  <text x="..." y="..." fill="{fg}" font-weight="..." font-style="..." text-decoration="...">
    {xml_escaped_text}
  </text>
  <!-- Cursor rect if visible -->
  <rect x="..." y="..." width="..." height="..." fill="{cursor_color}" opacity="0.7"/>
</svg>
```

## Font Specification

Use the font stack `"'Fira Code', 'Cascadia Code', 'JetBrains Mono', 'SF Mono', Menlo, Monaco, Consolas, 'Liberation Mono', 'Courier New', monospace"`. Character width = `font_size * 0.6`, line height = `font_size * 1.2`. Document that these are approximations and alignment depends on the viewer's available fonts.

## XML Escaping

All text content must be escaped: `<` -> `<`, `>` -> `>`, `&` -> `&`, `"` -> `"`. Implement a helper function `fn xml_escape(s: &str) -> String`.

## Edge Cases

- **Empty/blank lines:** No `<text>` element emitted for lines with only whitespace. Background rects still emitted if line has non-default background colors.
- **Cursor rendering:** If `cursor.visible` at target time, render a semi-transparent rect at cursor position.
- **Wide characters:** Use `char_width` from avt segments to compute correct x-offsets.
- **`--at` beyond duration:** Clamp to duration (same as `Player::seek()`), not an error.
- **Empty recording:** Render a blank terminal with default bg color.
- **No window chrome for v1:** Raw terminal content only.

## CLI Integration

Add `export` as a clap subcommand to `main.rs`, with `svg` as a sub-subcommand:

```
speedrun export svg <file.cast> -o <output.svg> [--at <seconds>] [--font-size <px>] [--force]
```

`--at` defaults to 0.0. `-o` is required. If output file exists without `--force`, error: "Output file already exists. Use --force to overwrite."

The `export` subcommand should be structured to allow adding `gif` and other formats later as sibling sub-subcommands.

## Tests

Tests go in `crates/speedrun-export/src/svg.rs` under `#[cfg(test)] mod tests`. Use inline test recordings (same `make_recording` helper pattern as search tests).

### Required test cases (minimum 10 tests):

#### XML structure tests

1. **`test_svg_starts_and_ends_correctly`** — Export a minimal recording at t=0. Verify the output starts with `<svg ` and contains `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"`, and ends with `</svg>`.
2. **`test_svg_has_background_rect`** — Export a minimal recording. Verify the output contains a `<rect` with `width="100%"` and `height="100%"` (the full-screen background fill).
3. **`test_svg_viewbox_dimensions`** — Export an 80x24 recording. Verify the `viewBox` dimensions match expected values: width = `80 * font_size * 0.6`, height = `24 * font_size * 1.2`.

#### XML escaping tests

4. **`test_xml_escape_function`** — Call `xml_escape("<hello>&\"world\"")` and verify output is `"<hello>&"world""`. This tests the helper directly.
5. **`test_svg_escapes_special_chars_in_content`** — Create a recording that outputs `<script>&alert</script>` via escape sequence. Export to SVG. Verify the output contains `<script>` and NOT the raw `<script>` tag. Parse with a simple check that no unescaped `<` appears in text content (only in SVG tags).

#### Color resolution tests

6. **`test_svg_fg_color_in_text_element`** — Create a recording that outputs red text (e.g., `\x1b[31mhello`). Export to SVG. Verify the `<text>` element has a `fill` attribute with the xterm red RGB value (as hex like `#cd0000`).
7. **`test_svg_bg_color_rect`** — Create a recording that outputs text with a blue background (`\x1b[44mhello`). Export to SVG. Verify a `<rect>` element exists for that segment with a `fill` matching xterm blue.

#### Text attribute tests

8. **`test_svg_bold_text_attribute`** — Create a recording with bold text (`\x1b[1mhello`). Export to SVG. Verify the `<text>` element has `font-weight="bold"`.
9. **`test_svg_italic_text_attribute`** — Create a recording with italic text (`\x1b[3mhello`). Verify `font-style="italic"` in the `<text>` element.

#### Edge case tests

10. **`test_svg_empty_recording`** — Export a recording with no events at t=0. Verify valid SVG is produced (starts with `<svg`, ends with `</svg>`, has background rect, no `<text>` elements).
11. **`test_svg_at_time_beyond_duration`** — Export with `at_time` set to 9999.0 on a recording that lasts 5 seconds. Verify it does not error and produces valid SVG showing the final frame.
12. **`test_svg_cursor_visible`** — Create a recording where the cursor is visible. Export to SVG with `show_cursor: true`. Verify the output contains a cursor `<rect>` element with `opacity` attribute.

#### Snapshot test (optional but recommended)

13. **`test_svg_output_snapshot`** — Use `insta::assert_snapshot!` to capture the full SVG output of a small (e.g., 10x3) recording with colored text. This catches unintended rendering regressions. Use a tiny terminal size to keep snapshots manageable.

### SVG validation approach

For structural validation, use string assertions (`.contains()`, `.starts_with()`, `.ends_with()`). Do NOT add an XML parsing dependency just for tests. If you want stronger validation, check that the output round-trips through a simple well-formedness check (matching `<` and `>` counts, etc.), but this is optional.

## Verification

```bash
cargo build --workspace
cargo test --workspace
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings
# Manual verification: speedrun export svg testdata/minimal_v2.cast -o /tmp/test.svg
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- `speedrun export svg testdata/minimal_v2.cast -o out.svg` produces a file
- Output is well-formed SVG (starts with `<svg`, ends with `</svg>`, proper XML namespace)
- Characters `<`, `>`, `&` in terminal content are correctly escaped
- Colors in SVG match palette resolution for indexed and RGB colors
- File size is reasonable (under 100KB for an 80x24 screen)
- `--at 9999` clamps to recording end, not an error
- `--at 0` on an empty recording produces a valid SVG with blank terminal
- Cursor rect is present in output when cursor is visible at the specified time
- Output file already exists without `--force` -> error with descriptive message
- `--force` overwrites existing file
- Exit code 0 on success, non-zero on failure
- Background rects emitted for non-default background colors
- `<text>` elements have correct `fill`, `font-weight`, `font-style`, `text-decoration` attributes matching pen attributes
- At least 10 unit tests covering SVG structure, XML escaping, color resolution, text attributes, and edge cases
