title: SUPERSEDED — See 7nx.4 and 7nx.5
description:
Render a full recording as an animated GIF. Includes CLI wiring.

## Dependencies (new crates)

- `gif` — GIF89a encoding
- `image` — pixel buffer manipulation (`RgbaImage`)
- `fontdue` — font rasterization (NOT `font8x8` — `fontdue` supports Unicode via embedded TTF, critical for box-drawing characters, arrows, and other common terminal glyphs)

## Font

Embed a monospace font with broad Unicode coverage. Recommended: embed a subset of a permissively-licensed monospace font (e.g., JetBrains Mono or Liberation Mono) as a compile-time `include_bytes!()`. The font must cover at minimum: ASCII (U+0020-U+007E), Latin-1 Supplement (U+00A0-U+00FF), Box Drawing (U+2500-U+257F), Block Elements (U+2580-U+259F), and common symbols (arrows, bullets). Characters outside the embedded font's coverage render as a fallback glyph (U+25A1 WHITE SQUARE). Document the supported Unicode ranges.

Default cell size: 8x16 pixels (width x height). Configurable via `--scale` (1x, 2x, 3x — multiplies cell dimensions).

## Frame Generation

1. Loop: `for t in (0.0..=duration).step_by(1.0 / fps)`
2. At each `t`, call `player.seek(t)` and render `player.screen()` to a pixel buffer
3. For each cell: resolve colors via Palette, render the glyph via `fontdue`, composite onto the frame buffer
4. Quantize the frame to 256 colors using NeuQuant algorithm (via `color_quant` crate or `gif` crate's built-in quantizer)
5. Encode the frame with the `gif` crate

## Frame Diffing

Compare each frame to the previous frame. For unchanged pixels, mark as transparent using GIF's disposal method (restore to previous). This significantly reduces file size. Required for reasonable file sizes — not optional.

## GIF-Specific Options

```rust
pub struct GifOptions {
    pub fps: u32,        // default: 10, max: 50 (GIF delay min is 2 centiseconds)
    pub scale: u32,      // default: 1 (multiplier for cell dimensions)
    pub loop_count: u16, // default: 0 (infinite loop)
    // Inherits ExportOptions for palette
}
```

## Edge Cases

- **Resize events:** Use the **initial** terminal dimensions for GIF frame size. If the recording resizes larger, crop to initial dimensions. If it resizes smaller, pad with default background. Document this limitation — resize handling is best-effort for v1.
- **GIF frame delay minimum:** Validate FPS <= 50. If user specifies `--fps 60`, error with "Maximum FPS for GIF is 50 (due to GIF timing resolution). Use --fps 50 or lower."
- **GIF looping:** Default infinite loop. `--loop 1` plays once. `--loop 0` (explicit) means infinite.
- **Empty recording:** Produce a single-frame GIF of the blank terminal.
- **Long recordings:** Print progress to stderr: `Rendering frame 150/3000...` (update every 100 frames or every 2 seconds). Recordings > 5 minutes: warn about potential large file size on stderr.
- **Memory:** Keep at most 2 frames in memory (current + previous for diffing). Stream frames to the encoder — don't buffer all frames before writing.
- **Effective time:** `Player::seek()` already operates on effective time, so idle compression is automatic.
- **Color quantization quality:** Use NeuQuant with sample factor 10 for quality/speed balance. If a frame has <= 256 unique colors, skip quantization (use exact palette).

## CLI Integration

```
speedrun export gif <file.cast> -o <output.gif> [--fps <N>] [--scale <N>] [--loop <N>] [--force]
```

`-o` is required. Output file collision: error without `--force`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `speedrun export gif testdata/minimal_v2.cast -o out.gif` produces a valid GIF89a file viewable in standard image viewers and web browsers
- Frame count approximately equals `ceil(duration * fps)` (+-1 for boundary)
- Non-ASCII characters (box-drawing, arrows) render as recognizable glyphs, not blank/corrupted (test with a recording containing TUI app output)
- Characters outside the font's coverage render as fallback glyph
- Colors match palette resolution (test: red text appears red in GIF)
- `--fps 60` -> error with descriptive message about GIF timing limits
- `--fps 10` on a 30-second recording produces ~300 frames
- Output file size for a 30-second 80x24 recording at 10 FPS is under 5MB
- Empty recording produces a single-frame GIF
- Progress output appears on stderr during encoding
- `--force` overwrites existing output file; without it, existing file -> error
- `--scale 2` doubles the pixel dimensions
- `--loop 1` produces a non-looping GIF; default produces infinite loop
- Frame diffing is active: file size is significantly smaller than frame_count x frame_size_uncompressed
- Memory usage stays bounded (does not grow with recording length)
- Recording with resize events: GIF maintains initial dimensions, content is cropped/padded as needed after resize

title: 9.4a — Font infrastructure and pixel renderer
description:
Set up the font infrastructure and pixel renderer for GIF export. This ticket creates the screen-to-pixel-buffer pipeline. GIF encoding and CLI wiring are in ticket 9.4b.

## Files to Create

- `crates/speedrun-export/src/font.rs` — font loading, glyph rasterization
- `crates/speedrun-export/src/renderer.rs` — screen-to-pixel-buffer rendering
- `crates/speedrun-export/fonts/` — directory for the embedded font file

## Dependencies to Add (to speedrun-export/Cargo.toml)

- `fontdue` — font rasterization from TTF/OTF bytes
- `image` — pixel buffer manipulation (`RgbaImage`)

## Font Setup

Embed **JetBrains Mono Regular** (OFL-1.1 license, permissive, widely used). Download the TTF file from the GitHub releases: https://github.com/JetBrains/JetBrainsMono/releases — use the `JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf` file from the latest release.

Place the font file at `crates/speedrun-export/fonts/JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf`.

Load at compile time:
```rust
const FONT_BYTES: &[u8] = include_bytes!("../fonts/JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf");
```

Parse with `fontdue::Font::from_bytes(FONT_BYTES, fontdue::FontSettings::default())`.

**Unicode coverage:** JetBrains Mono covers ASCII, Latin-1, Box Drawing (U+2500-U+257F), Block Elements (U+2580-U+259F), arrows, mathematical symbols, and many other ranges. Characters outside the font's coverage render as U+25A1 (WHITE SQUARE) fallback glyph. If the fallback glyph itself is missing, render a filled rectangle.

## Pixel Renderer

Implement a `ScreenRenderer` struct:

```rust
pub struct ScreenRenderer {
    font: fontdue::Font,
    cell_width: u32,    // pixels per cell column
    cell_height: u32,   // pixels per cell row
    palette: Palette,   // from ticket 9.1
}

impl ScreenRenderer {
    pub fn new(palette: Palette, scale: u32) -> Self;

    /// Render the current screen state to a pixel buffer.
    /// `screen` is from `player.screen()` (i.e., `vt.view()`).
    /// `cursor` is from `player.cursor()`.
    /// Returns an RGBA image buffer.
    pub fn render_frame(
        &self,
        screen: &[avt::Line],
        cursor: &CursorState,
        width: u16,
        height: u16,
    ) -> image::RgbaImage;
}
```

Default cell size: 8x16 pixels (width x height) at scale=1. Scale multiplies both dimensions.

**Rendering per cell:**
1. Resolve fg/bg colors via `Palette::resolve_cell_colors(pen)` (handles inverse, bold brightening)
2. Fill the cell rectangle with the background color
3. Rasterize the character glyph with `fontdue::Font::rasterize(char, font_size)` at the appropriate size
4. Composite the glyph pixels onto the cell rectangle using the foreground color (glyph coverage as alpha)
5. Handle cursor: if `cursor.visible` and position matches, render a filled rectangle or invert the cell colors

**Edge cases:**
- Wide characters (CJK): rasterize at double cell width, skip the next cell
- Space characters: skip glyph rasterization, just fill background
- Missing glyphs: render fallback (filled rectangle with fg color)

## Tests

Tests go in `crates/speedrun-export/src/renderer.rs` under `#[cfg(test)] mod tests`. To create test screen content, use `avt::Vt` instances with known content.

### Required test cases (minimum 10 tests):

#### Image dimension tests

1. **`test_render_frame_dimensions_default_scale`** — Create a `ScreenRenderer` with `scale=1`. Render a frame for an 80x24 terminal. Verify the returned `RgbaImage` has dimensions `(80 * 8, 24 * 16)` = `(640, 384)`.
2. **`test_render_frame_dimensions_scale_2`** — Same but with `scale=2`. Verify dimensions are `(80 * 16, 24 * 32)` = `(1280, 768)`.
3. **`test_render_frame_small_terminal`** — Render a 10x3 terminal at scale 1. Verify dimensions are `(80, 48)`.

#### Pixel content tests (non-trivial rendering)

4. **`test_render_text_produces_non_background_pixels`** — Create a Vt with "hello" written at row 0. Render the frame. Sample pixels in the area where "h" should be rendered (approximately row 0, columns 0-7 in pixels). Verify that at least some pixels differ from the background color. Specifically: collect unique pixel values in the first cell rect, assert `unique_colors.len() > 1` (background + foreground glyph pixels).
5. **`test_render_blank_screen_is_uniform`** — Render a Vt with no content written. Verify ALL pixels in the image are the default background color (`RGB8::new(30, 30, 30)` → RGBA `[30, 30, 30, 255]`). This confirms no stray glyph rendering.
6. **`test_render_colored_text_uses_correct_fg`** — Create a Vt and feed `\x1b[31mX` (red "X"). Render the frame. Sample non-background pixels in the cell for column 0. Verify they use the red foreground color (the pixel RGB should match xterm red `(205, 0, 0)` or close to it).
7. **`test_render_colored_background`** — Create a Vt and feed `\x1b[42m ` (green background space). Render the frame. Sample pixels in that cell. Verify they match xterm green background color (all pixels in that cell should be the green color since it's a space with colored background).

#### Fallback glyph test

8. **`test_render_missing_glyph_fallback`** — Create a Vt and feed a character known to be outside JetBrains Mono's coverage (e.g., U+1F600 😀 or a rare CJK character). Render the frame. Verify the cell is NOT entirely background color (the fallback glyph — either U+25A1 or a filled rect — should produce some foreground pixels). Check: `pixels_in_cell.iter().any(|p| p != &bg_pixel)`.

#### Cursor test

9. **`test_render_cursor_visible`** — Create a Vt with cursor visible at position (0, 0). Render the frame with a `CursorState { visible: true, col: 0, row: 0 }`. Sample pixels at the cursor position. Verify they differ from a render with `CursorState { visible: false, .. }` at the same position. Compare pixel data at the cursor cell: `pixels_with_cursor != pixels_without_cursor`.

#### Font loading test

10. **`test_font_loads_successfully`** — Verify that `fontdue::Font::from_bytes(FONT_BYTES, ...)` succeeds (does not panic or error). This catches issues with the embedded font file being corrupted or missing.
11. **`test_font_rasterizes_ascii`** — Rasterize the character 'A' at size 16.0. Verify the returned bitmap is non-empty (width > 0, height > 0) and contains non-zero values (glyph has actual content).

### Test helpers

To create a VT screen with known content for rendering tests:

```rust
fn make_test_screen(content: &str, width: u16, height: u16) -> avt::Vt {
    let mut vt = avt::Vt::new(width as usize, height as usize);
    vt.feed_str(content);
    vt
}
```

To sample pixels from a specific cell position:

```rust
fn sample_cell_pixels(img: &image::RgbaImage, col: u32, row: u32, cell_w: u32, cell_h: u32) -> Vec<image::Rgba<u8>> {
    let x_start = col * cell_w;
    let y_start = row * cell_h;
    let mut pixels = Vec::new();
    for y in y_start..y_start + cell_h {
        for x in x_start..x_start + cell_w {
            pixels.push(*img.get_pixel(x, y));
        }
    }
    pixels
}
```

## Verification

```bash
cargo build --workspace
cargo test --workspace
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- `ScreenRenderer::render_frame()` produces an `RgbaImage` with correct dimensions (`width * cell_width * scale` x `height * cell_height * scale`)
- Text characters are visible in the rendered image (glyph pixels differ from background)
- Background colors match palette resolution
- Foreground colors match palette resolution
- Fallback glyph renders for characters outside font coverage (cell is not entirely background)
- Font file exists at `crates/speedrun-export/fonts/JetBrainsMono-Regular.ttf`
- License file for the font is present (OFL-1.1)
- Scale=2 doubles the pixel dimensions
- Blank screen renders as uniform background color
- Cursor rendering produces visible pixels at cursor position
- At least 10 unit tests covering dimensions, pixel content, fallback glyphs, cursor, and font loading

title: 9.4b — GIF encoding and CLI
description:
GIF encoding, frame diffing, and CLI wiring for animated GIF export. Depends on ticket 9.4a (font + pixel renderer).

## Files to Modify/Create

- `crates/speedrun-export/src/gif.rs` — GIF encoding pipeline
- `crates/speedrun-export/src/lib.rs` — export `pub mod gif`
- `crates/speedrun/src/main.rs` — add `export gif` subcommand
- `crates/speedrun/Cargo.toml` — add `speedrun-export` dependency (if not already added by 9.2)

## Dependencies to Add (to speedrun-export/Cargo.toml)

- `gif` — GIF89a encoding
- `color_quant` — NeuQuant color quantization (256-color palette generation)

## GIF Export Function

```rust
pub fn export_gif(
    player: &mut Player,
    options: &GifOptions,
    writer: impl Write,
    progress: Option<&dyn Fn(usize, usize)>,  // (current_frame, total_frames)
) -> Result<(), ExportError>;
```

## GIF-Specific Options

```rust
pub struct GifOptions {
    pub fps: u32,          // default: 10, max: 50
    pub scale: u32,        // default: 1
    pub loop_count: u16,   // default: 0 (infinite)
    pub export: ExportOptions,  // palette config from 9.1
}
```

## Frame Generation Loop

1. Validate FPS: if `fps > 50`, return error "Maximum FPS for GIF is 50 (due to GIF timing resolution of 10ms). Use --fps 50 or lower."
2. Compute total frames: `ceil(player.duration() * fps)`
3. Compute GIF frame delay: `100 / fps` centiseconds
4. Create `ScreenRenderer` from ticket 9.4a
5. Loop `for frame_idx in 0..total_frames`:
   a. `player.seek(frame_idx as f64 / fps as f64)`
   b. `renderer.render_frame(player.screen(), player.cursor(), width, height)` -> `RgbaImage`
   c. Quantize to 256 colors using NeuQuant (sample factor 10). If frame has <= 256 unique colors, use exact palette.
   d. Apply frame diffing (see below)
   e. Encode frame with `gif` crate
   f. Call progress callback if provided
6. Use initial terminal dimensions (`player.size()` at time 0) for all frames. If the recording resizes larger, crop. If smaller, pad with default bg.

## Frame Diffing

Compare each frame's quantized pixel buffer to the previous frame. For unchanged pixels, set them to a designated transparent color index. Use GIF disposal method `Previous` or `Background` so unchanged regions show through from the prior frame. This is required — not optional — for reasonable file sizes.

## Memory Management

Keep at most 2 pixel buffers in memory (current + previous for diffing). Stream encoded frames directly to the writer. Do NOT accumulate all frames before writing.

## CLI Integration

Add `export gif` as a clap subcommand:

```
speedrun export gif <file.cast> -o <output.gif> [--fps <N>] [--scale <N>] [--loop <N>] [--force]
```

- `-o` is required
- `--fps` defaults to 10, validated <= 50
- `--scale` defaults to 1
- `--loop` defaults to 0 (infinite)
- If output file exists without `--force`, error: "Output file already exists. Use --force to overwrite."
- Print progress to stderr: `Rendering frame 150/3000...` (update every 100 frames)
- Recordings > 5 minutes: warn on stderr about potential large file size

## Edge Cases

- **Empty recording (0 events, 0 duration):** Produce a single-frame GIF of the blank terminal
- **GIF looping:** Write Netscape Application Extension. `loop_count=0` means infinite, `loop_count=1` means play once
- **Resize events in recording:** Use initial dimensions for all frames. Crop/pad as needed. Document this limitation in a comment.

## Tests

Tests go in `crates/speedrun-export/src/gif.rs` under `#[cfg(test)] mod tests`. Use inline test recordings. For tests that produce GIF output, write to a `Vec<u8>` buffer and verify the result without requiring filesystem access.

### Required test cases (minimum 10 tests):

#### GIF format validation tests

1. **`test_gif_magic_bytes`** — Export a minimal recording to a `Vec<u8>`. Verify the first 6 bytes are `b"GIF89a"` (the GIF89a magic header).
2. **`test_gif_non_empty_output`** — Export a minimal recording. Verify the output length is > 100 bytes (a valid GIF has header, LSD, color table, at least one frame, and trailer).

#### Frame count tests

3. **`test_gif_frame_count_matches_fps`** — Export a 3-second recording at 10 FPS. Parse the GIF output to count frames. Expected: `ceil(3.0 * 10) = 30` frames (±1). To count GIF frames, search the output bytes for the GIF image separator byte `0x2C` — each frame starts with this byte. Count occurrences.
4. **`test_gif_single_frame_empty_recording`** — Export a recording with 0 events (0 duration). Verify the output contains exactly 1 frame (the blank terminal). Count `0x2C` byte occurrences.

#### FPS validation tests

5. **`test_gif_rejects_fps_over_50`** — Call `export_gif` with `fps: 60`. Verify it returns `Err` with a message containing "50" (the maximum). Do NOT check for panic — this must be a proper error return.
6. **`test_gif_accepts_fps_50`** — Call `export_gif` with `fps: 50`. Verify it returns `Ok` (the maximum valid value is accepted).

#### Frame diffing test

7. **`test_gif_frame_diffing_reduces_size`** — Create a recording where most of the screen is static (e.g., a single character changes). Export at 10 FPS. Compare the output size to a rough upper bound: `frames * width * height * scale * 3` (uncompressed). The actual GIF should be significantly smaller (at least 50% smaller than the naive upper bound). This doesn't test the exact diffing algorithm but confirms that some form of frame optimization is active.

#### Scale test

8. **`test_gif_scale_doubles_dimensions`** — Export the same recording at `scale=1` and `scale=2`. Parse the GIF Logical Screen Descriptor (bytes 6-9: width as little-endian u16, bytes 8-9: height as little-endian u16) from each. Verify the scale=2 dimensions are exactly 2x the scale=1 dimensions.

#### Progress callback test

9. **`test_gif_progress_callback_called`** — Export a recording with `progress: Some(&callback)` where the callback records `(current, total)` tuples into a `RefCell<Vec<(usize, usize)>>`. Verify the callback was called at least once and that `total` is consistent across calls. Also verify the last call has `current` close to `total`.

#### Error handling tests

10. **`test_gif_export_returns_result_not_panic`** — Export a valid recording. Verify the return type is `Ok(())`. This ensures the function uses `Result` properly and doesn't `unwrap()` internally.

### GIF frame counting approach

To count frames in GIF output without adding a GIF decoding dependency:

```rust
fn count_gif_frames(data: &[u8]) -> usize {
    // GIF frames start with Image Separator byte 0x2C
    data.iter().filter(|&&b| b == 0x2C).count()
}
```

Note: This is an approximation — `0x2C` could theoretically appear in compressed data. For test recordings with simple content, this is reliable. If more precision is needed, implement a minimal GIF block parser that follows the GIF89a spec (skip extension blocks, count image descriptors).

### GIF dimension parsing

```rust
fn gif_dimensions(data: &[u8]) -> (u16, u16) {
    // Logical Screen Descriptor starts at byte 6
    let width = u16::from_le_bytes([data[6], data[7]]);
    let height = u16::from_le_bytes([data[8], data[9]]);
    (width, height)
}
```

### What is unit-testable vs manual-only

| Acceptance Criterion | Testable | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Valid GIF89a file | Unit test | Check magic bytes |
| Frame count ≈ duration × FPS | Unit test | Count 0x2C bytes |
| --fps 60 → error | Unit test | Check Result::Err |
| Output size < naive upper bound | Unit test | Size comparison |
| Scale doubles dimensions | Unit test | Parse GIF LSD |
| Progress callback fires | Unit test | Record calls |
| Colors match palette | Manual | Visual inspection of GIF |
| Memory stays bounded | Manual | Monitor RSS during long export |
| --force overwrites file | Integration test (or manual) | Needs filesystem |
| --loop produces correct GIF | Manual | View in browser |
| Resize crop/pad | Manual | Recording with resize events |

## Verification

```bash
cargo build --workspace
cargo test --workspace
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings
# Manual: speedrun export gif testdata/real_session.cast -o /tmp/test.gif && file /tmp/test.gif
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- `speedrun export gif testdata/minimal_v2.cast -o out.gif` produces a valid GIF89a file (`file out.gif` reports "GIF image data")
- Frame count approximately equals `ceil(duration * fps)` (±1)
- `--fps 60` -> error with descriptive message
- `--fps 10` on a 30-second recording produces ~300 frames
- Output file size for a 30-second 80x24 recording at 10 FPS is under 5MB
- Empty recording produces a single-frame GIF
- Progress output appears on stderr during encoding
- `--force` overwrites existing output file; without it, existing file -> error
- `--scale 2` doubles the pixel dimensions
- `--loop 1` produces a non-looping GIF; default produces infinite loop
- Frame diffing is active: file size is significantly smaller than without diffing
- Memory usage stays bounded (does not grow with recording length)
- Colors match palette resolution (red text appears red)
- At least 10 unit tests covering GIF format validation, frame count, FPS validation, frame diffing, scale, and progress callback

title: Epic 1.2 — Asciicast Parser
description:
Parse asciicast v2 and v3 files into an in-memory event list with typed events, eager resize parsing, and strict error handling.

## Context

The parser reads from `impl Read` (wrapped in `BufReader` internally). V2 timestamps are absolute; V3 timestamps are relative intervals normalized to absolute during parsing.

### Key design decisions

- **Strict error handling**: fails on first malformed event line. Lenient/skip mode deferred to Epic 4.1.
- **Eager resize parsing**: `"r"` events with `"COLSxROWS"` data are parsed into `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }` at parse time — no stringly-typed data passed downstream.
- **Monotonicity validation**: V2 timestamps must be non-decreasing, V3 intervals must be non-negative.
- **Markers extracted twice**: present in the events vec (for replay ordering) AND in a separate `markers: Vec<Marker>` (for player navigation API).

### Error types

- `EmptyFile` — zero bytes / no lines
- `InvalidHeader` — first line isn't valid JSON header (with source error)
- `UnsupportedVersion` — version not 2 or 3
- `MissingField` — header missing width/height
- `InvalidEvent` — malformed event line (with line number)
- `InvalidResize` — bad `"COLSxROWS"` format
- `NotUtf8` / `Io` — I/O and encoding errors

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/parser.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.2.1 — Parser types and error handling
- 1.2.2 — Parse implementation
- 1.2.3 — Parser tests
notes:
## Update: Unknown event types are skipped, not errored

Analysis of a real v3 recording revealed event type `"x"` (exit) which is not in the o/i/m/r set. The parser now **skips unknown event types silently** rather than erroring. This ensures real-world recordings parse successfully.

Also: V3 headers use `term.cols`/`term.rows` instead of top-level `width`/`height`. The `RawHeader` serde struct handles both formats.

title: 1.2.1 — Parser types and error handling
description:
Define all parser types, the error enum, and wire the module into lib.rs. No parsing logic yet.

## Cross-cutting conventions

- **Derives**: `Header`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `Event`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `EventType`: `Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize`. `EventData`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `Marker`: `Debug, Clone, Serialize`. `Recording`: `Debug, Serialize`.
- **`impl Read` API**: The public `parse()` function signature accepts `impl Read`, wraps in `BufReader` internally. Declare the signature as a stub (returning `todo!()`) so the API shape is visible.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/parser.rs` with types:

   ```rust
   #[derive(Debug, Serialize)]
   pub struct Recording {
       pub header: Header,
       pub events: Vec<Event>,
       pub markers: Vec<Marker>,  // Extracted from events for convenience
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub struct Header {
       pub version: u8,           // 2 or 3
       pub width: u16,
       pub height: u16,
       pub timestamp: Option<u64>,
       pub idle_time_limit: Option<f64>,
       pub title: Option<String>,
       pub env: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize)]
   pub enum EventType {
       Output,
       Input,
       Marker,
       Resize,
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub struct Event {
       pub time: f64,              // Raw absolute timestamp (seconds)
       pub event_type: EventType,
       pub data: EventData,
   }

   /// Typed event data. Resize is eagerly parsed into structured dimensions
   /// so downstream consumers never parse "COLSxROWS" strings.
   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub enum EventData {
       Text(String),
       Resize { cols: u16, rows: u16 },
   }

   #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
   pub struct Marker {
       pub time: f64,    // Raw absolute timestamp (effective time from TimeMap)
       pub label: String,
   }
   ```

2. Define `ParseError`:
   ```rust
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub enum ParseError {
       EmptyFile,
       InvalidHeader { line: String, source: serde_json::Error },
       UnsupportedVersion { version: u64 },
       MissingField { field: &'static str },
       InvalidEvent { line_number: usize, content: String, reason: String },
       InvalidResize { line_number: usize, data: String },
       NotUtf8 { source: std::io::Error },
       Io { source: std::io::Error },
   }
   ```
   Implement `std::fmt::Display` and `std::error::Error` for `ParseError`.

3. Declare a private serde-deserialization struct for the raw header:
   ```rust
   #[derive(Deserialize)]
   struct RawHeader {
       version: u64,              // u64 to detect unsupported versions like 99
       width: Option<u16>,
       height: Option<u16>,
       timestamp: Option<u64>,
       idle_time_limit: Option<f64>,
       title: Option<String>,
       env: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   }
   ```

4. Declare the public API stub:
   ```rust
   pub fn parse(reader: impl std::io::Read) -> Result<Recording, ParseError> {
       todo!()
   }
   ```

5. Add `pub mod parser;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export key types: `pub use parser::{Recording, Header, Event, EventType, EventData, Marker, ParseError, parse};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `cargo build -p speedrun-core` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] All types have the specified derives
- [ ] `ParseError` implements `Display` and `Error`
- [ ] `parse()` stub exists with the correct signature
- [ ] `RawHeader` is private (not exported)
- [ ] Types are exported from `lib.rs`
notes:
## Codebase context

**Current state of files you will modify:**

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/lib.rs` contains only: `// speedrun-core library - Terminal session player core engine` (1 line, no modules, no imports).
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml` already has `serde.workspace = true` (with derive feature enabled at workspace level) and `serde_json.workspace = true`. No new dependencies needed.
- `parser.rs` does not exist yet — create it.

**Other epics may modify `lib.rs` concurrently** (snapshot.rs and timemap.rs are being built in parallel). Just add your `pub mod parser;` line and re-exports. Do not assume the file is empty or that you own it exclusively.

**Serde usage**: The workspace declares `serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }`, so `#[derive(Serialize)]` works with just `use serde::Serialize;`. For `Deserialize` (used on `RawHeader`): `use serde::Deserialize;`.

**The `todo!()` stub** is intentional — the implementation comes in ticket 1.2.2. The stub lets the types compile and be visible in the API while deferring logic. `cargo test` will not call `parse()` so the `todo!()` will not panic.

## Verification commands

Run these before marking done:
```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```
## IMPORTANT: V3 header format differs from V2

The real v3 asciicast format nests dimensions under `term`:

**V2 header**: `{"version": 2, "width": 80, "height": 24, ...}`
**V3 header**: `{"version": 3, "term": {"cols": 188, "rows": 50, "type": "xterm-ghostty", ...}, ...}`

The `RawHeader` serde struct must handle both. Recommended approach:

```rust
#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct RawHeader {
    version: u64,
    // V2 fields (top-level)
    width: Option<u16>,
    height: Option<u16>,
    // V3 fields (nested under term)
    term: Option<RawTerm>,
    // Shared
    timestamp: Option<u64>,
    idle_time_limit: Option<f64>,
    title: Option<String>,
    env: Option<serde_json::Value>,
}

#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct RawTerm {
    cols: Option<u16>,
    rows: Option<u16>,
    #[serde(rename = "type")]
    term_type: Option<String>,
    version: Option<String>,
    // ignore theme and other fields
}
```

When converting to public `Header`:
- V2: use `width`/`height` directly
- V3: use `term.cols`/`term.rows`
- Validation: after version check, verify the appropriate fields are present. MissingField error should say "width" (v2) or "term.cols" (v3) depending on version.

title: 1.2.2 — Parse implementation
description:
Implement the `parse()` function: header parsing, event parsing, V3 normalization, resize eager parsing, monotonicity validation.

## Tasks

1. **`parse()` entry point**: Accept `impl Read`, wrap in `BufReader`, read lines, delegate to header and event parsing helpers.

2. **Header parsing**:
   - Read first line. If EOF → `ParseError::EmptyFile`.
   - Binary garbage detection: if the first line contains non-UTF8 bytes, or if the first 32 bytes contain non-printable characters (excluding \t, \n, \r), return an early error (`NotUtf8` or `InvalidHeader`).
   - Deserialize via serde into `RawHeader` (the private struct from 1.2.1). On failure → `InvalidHeader { line, source }`.
   - Validate `version` is 2 or 3 → else `UnsupportedVersion { version }`.
   - Validate `width` is present → else `MissingField { field: "width" }`.
   - Validate `height` is present → else `MissingField { field: "height" }`.
   - Convert `RawHeader` to public `Header` (narrow version from u64 to u8).

3. **Event parsing**:
   - Read subsequent lines. Track line numbers (starting from 1 for the header).
   - Skip empty/whitespace-only lines gracefully.
   - Parse each line as `serde_json::Value`. If not valid JSON → `InvalidEvent { line_number, content, reason: "invalid JSON" }`.
   - Expect a JSON array of exactly 3 elements → else `InvalidEvent` with reason "expected array of 3 elements".
   - Extract timestamp: must be a JSON number, non-null. Convert to f64. If null or non-numeric → `InvalidEvent` with reason "invalid timestamp".
   - Validate timestamp/interval is non-negative → else `InvalidEvent` with reason "negative timestamp".
   - Extract event type string: map `"o"→Output`, `"i"→Input`, `"m"→Marker`, `"r"→Resize`. Unknown type → `InvalidEvent` with reason "unknown event type: X".
   - Extract data: must be a JSON string → else `InvalidEvent`.

4. **V3 timestamp normalization**: If `header.version == 3`, timestamps are relative intervals. Accumulate absolute time:
   ```
   absolute_time = 0.0
   for each event:
       absolute_time += interval
       event.time = absolute_time
   ```
   The first event's interval is relative to t=0.

5. **Monotonicity check (V2 only)**: For version 2, verify each event's timestamp >= the previous event's timestamp. If not → `InvalidEvent { line_number, content, reason: "timestamps must be non-decreasing" }`. (V3 intervals are already validated as non-negative in step 3.)

6. **Resize eager parsing**: If event type is `Resize`, parse the data string:
   - Split on `'x'`. Expect exactly 2 parts.
   - Parse each part as `u16`.
   - On failure → `InvalidResize { line_number, data }`.
   - Store as `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`.
   - **Note**: the data format is `"COLSxROWS"` (cols first, rows second). The xtwinops escape sequence uses the opposite order (`rows;cols`) — but that's the indexer's concern, not the parser's. The parser stores the parsed values by name.

7. **Marker extraction**: If event type is `Marker`, push `Marker { time, label: data_string }` to the markers vec. The event also goes into the events vec with `EventData::Text(label)`.

8. **All other events**: Store `EventData::Text(data_string)`.

9. Return `Recording { header, events, markers }`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `parse()` works end-to-end for all 8 valid testdata files (verify in next ticket)
- [ ] V3 timestamps are normalized to absolute
- [ ] Resize data is eagerly parsed into `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`
- [ ] Markers appear in both `events` vec and `markers` vec
- [ ] V2 non-monotonic timestamps produce `InvalidEvent`
- [ ] V3 negative intervals produce `InvalidEvent`
- [ ] Empty/whitespace-only lines are skipped
- [ ] Line numbers in errors are correct (1-indexed, header is line 1)
- [ ] Strict mode: first malformed event line stops parsing and returns error
notes:
## Codebase context

Ticket 1.2.1 will have already created `parser.rs` with all types, `ParseError`, `RawHeader`, and a `parse()` stub returning `todo!()`. You are replacing the `todo!()` with the real implementation.

## UTF-8 and binary garbage handling — clarification

`BufReader::lines()` returns `io::Result<String>`. If the underlying bytes are not valid UTF-8, `lines()` returns `Err(io::Error { kind: InvalidData, ... })`. This means:

- **You do NOT need to pre-read raw bytes** for binary garbage detection. Just attempt to read the first line via `lines().next()`. If it returns `Err(e)` with `e.kind() == ErrorKind::InvalidData`, map it to `ParseError::NotUtf8 { source: e }`.
- If it returns `Ok(line)`, proceed with JSON parsing.
- The `binary_garbage.cast` test file will trigger this path naturally.

So the actual flow is:
```rust
let reader = BufReader::new(reader);
let mut lines = reader.lines();
let first_line = match lines.next() {
    None => return Err(ParseError::EmptyFile),
    Some(Err(e)) => /* check e.kind() for InvalidData → NotUtf8, else Io */,
    Some(Ok(line)) => line,
};
// Now parse `first_line` as header JSON...
```

## Verification commands

After implementing, smoke-test with at least one file:
```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
# Quick smoke test (will be fully tested in 1.2.3):
cargo test -p speedrun-core
```
## IMPORTANT: Two changes from real-world recording analysis

### 1. V3 header parsing

V3 headers nest dimensions under `term`:
```json
{"version": 3, "term": {"cols": 188, "rows": 50, "type": "xterm-ghostty"}, "timestamp": 1772729753, "env": {"SHELL": "/bin/zsh"}}
```

When parsing the header, after detecting version:
- **V2**: read `width` and `height` from top level
- **V3**: read `term.cols` and `term.rows` from nested `term` object

Both produce the same `Header` struct (which has `width` and `height` fields).

### 2. Unknown event types are SKIPPED, not errored

Real-world v3 recordings contain event types like `"x"` (exit) that our parser does not handle. The parser should **skip unknown event types silently** rather than returning `InvalidEvent`. This is a pragmatic choice — real recordings must parse successfully.

Change from the original plan:
- **Before**: Unknown event type → `InvalidEvent` with reason "unknown event type: X"
- **After**: Unknown event type → skip the event, do not add to events vec, continue parsing

The synthetic "unknown event type" test in 1.2.3 should verify the event is skipped and parsing continues (not that it errors).

## Invalid file tests

   - `invalid/empty_file.cast` → `ParseError::EmptyFile`
   - `invalid/no_header.cast` → `ParseError::InvalidHeader`
   - `invalid/bad_json.cast` → `ParseError::InvalidHeader` (serde error in source)
   - `invalid/bad_version.cast` → `ParseError::UnsupportedVersion { version: 99 }`
   - `invalid/missing_fields.cast` → `ParseError::MissingField`
   - `invalid/bad_event.cast` → `ParseError::InvalidEvent` with `line_number: 2` (first bad line: `[0.5, "o"]`). Strict mode: parsing stops here.
   - `invalid/binary_garbage.cast` → error on first line (either `NotUtf8` or `InvalidHeader`)
   - `invalid/truncated.cast` → `ParseError::InvalidEvent` for the truncated line

## Synthetic edge case tests (in-memory, not from files)

11. **Resize bad format**: Test at least 2 malformed resize data strings (e.g., `"120"` missing separator, `"abcxdef"` non-numeric). Both should produce `InvalidResize`.

title: 3.5 — Phase 3 integration and acceptance
description:
## Summary

Merge the three parallel epic branches, resolve conflicts, and verify all Phase 3 acceptance criteria are met. Fix any interaction bugs between controls visibility, help overlay, and navigation.

## Context

Phase 3 was developed across three parallel epics in separate worktrees:
- Epic 3.1+3.2 (controls bar widget + behavior)
- Epic 3.3 (navigation + speed)
- Epic 3.4 (help overlay)

All three epics modified `app.rs` (new state fields, new match arms in `handle_action`, render logic). They also share the `ui.rs` module (new widgets). The foundation ticket ensured structural changes (Action enum, handle_key refactor) were shared, but additive changes in each branch need conflict resolution.

## Tasks

### 1. Merge branches

Merge each epic's branch into main (or a Phase 3 integration branch). Expected conflicts:
- `app.rs`: `App` struct fields (additive — combine all fields), `handle_action` match arms (additive — combine all arms), `render()` method (each epic added different rendering), `compute_timeout` (controls epic updated this)
- `ui.rs`: new widget imports/modules (additive)
- `main.rs`: should have minimal conflicts

### 2. Resolve interaction behaviors

Verify these cross-epic interactions work correctly after merge:

**Help overlay + controls visibility:**
- When help is shown, playback pauses → controls should become visible (per controls behavior rules)
- When help is dismissed and playback resumes → controls auto-hide timer starts
- Help renders on top of controls bar (rendering order in `render()`)

**Speed/navigation + controls visibility:**
- Speed changes show controls (from epic 3.3) using the visibility mechanism from epic 3.2
- All navigation actions show controls using the same mechanism

**Esc key context:**
- Help visible → Esc dismisses help (from epic 3.4)
- Help not visible → Esc quits (existing behavior)

**Action blocking during help:**
- Speed/navigation actions blocked while help is visible (from epic 3.4)

### 3. Run full quality gates

```bash
cargo build --workspace
cargo test --workspace
cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

Fix any compilation errors, test failures, or lint warnings from the merge.

### 4. Manual smoke testing

Verify against Phase 3 acceptance criteria with an actual .cast file:

- [ ] Controls bar displays correctly with all elements (icon, time, progress, speed, markers)
- [ ] Controls auto-hide after 2s during playback, show on pause/interaction/end
- [ ] Tab toggles controls; --no-controls starts hidden
- [ ] All keybindings from the spec are functional

- [ ] Marker navigation jumps correctly (test with `with_markers.cast`)
- [ ] Percentage jumps (0-9 keys) land at correct positions
- [ ] Help overlay shows/dismisses correctly, pauses/resumes playback
- [ ] Controls bar adapts responsively to narrow terminals (resize terminal to verify)

### 5. Fix integration issues

Any bugs found during verification get fixed in this ticket. Common issues to watch for:
- Rendering order: help overlay must be last (on top of everything)
- Controls visibility state not accounting for help overlay being active
- Auto-hide timer interfering with help overlay state
- Speed display not updating on controls bar after speed change
- Duplicate `needs_redraw` or missing redraw triggers

Epic 1.4 — Keyframe Indexer
description:
Build the keyframe index that enables instant seeking. Replay all events through `avt`, capturing `TerminalSnapshot`s every 5 seconds of effective time. The resulting `KeyframeIndex` supports O(log n) lookup for the seek engine.

## Context

The indexer is the bridge between parsing/time-mapping (done) and seeking/playback (next). It depends on all three completed modules:
- `parser` — `Recording`, `Event`, `EventType`, `EventData`
- `timemap` — `TimeMap` for effective timestamps
- `snapshot` — `TerminalSnapshot`, `create_vt`

### Key design decisions

- `KEYFRAME_INTERVAL = 5.0` seconds of effective time (constant, not configurable for v1).
- Always capture a "keyframe 0" at effective time 0 (initial terminal state before any events), so seeking to start is instant. If the recording has zero events, the index is empty.
- Only output events feed to the Vt via `feed_str()`. Input and marker events are skipped (no terminal state change).
- Resize events resize the Vt by feeding the xtwinops escape sequence `\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t`. **Note the parameter order**: rows then cols — opposite of `EventData::Resize` which stores `{ cols, rows }`. See `snapshot.rs:240` for the validated pattern.
- `Keyframe.event_index` = index of the first event NOT included in the snapshot (i.e., the first event to replay after restoring this keyframe).

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` (new)
- Updates to `lib.rs`

## Tickets

- 1.4.1 — Keyframe types and index builder
- 1.4.2 — Keyframe index tests

title: 1.4.1 — Keyframe types and index builder
description:
Define the keyframe types and implement the index builder that replays events through avt, capturing snapshots at 5-second effective-time intervals.

## Tasks

1. Create `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` with types:

   ```rust
   /// Interval between keyframes in effective time (seconds).
   pub const KEYFRAME_INTERVAL: f64 = 5.0;

   /// A snapshot of terminal state at a specific effective time.
   #[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   pub struct Keyframe {
       /// Effective timestamp (with idle limit applied).
       pub time: f64,
       /// Index of the first event NOT included in this snapshot.
       /// To resume playback from this keyframe, start replaying from
       /// `events[event_index]`.
       pub event_index: usize,
       /// Serialized terminal state.
       pub snapshot: TerminalSnapshot,
   }

   /// Index of keyframes for O(log n) seek lookup.
   #[derive(Debug)]
   pub struct KeyframeIndex {
       keyframes: Vec<Keyframe>,
   }
   ```

2. Implement `KeyframeIndex::build(recording: &Recording, time_map: &TimeMap) -> Self`:

   - If the recording has no events, return an empty index.
   - Create a `Vt` at header dimensions via `create_vt(width as usize, height as usize)`.
   - Capture the initial keyframe ("keyframe 0"): **`time = 0.0`** (the start of the timeline, before any events), `event_index = 0`, snapshot of the fresh Vt.
   - Set `next_keyframe_time = 0.0 + KEYFRAME_INTERVAL` (i.e., 5.0).
   - Iterate through all events by index `i`:
     - **Before processing** the event, check if `effective_time(i) >= next_keyframe_time`. If so, capture a keyframe with the current Vt state, `event_index = i`, `time = effective_time(i)`. Then **advance `next_keyframe_time` past the current event** to avoid double-captures on subsequent events:
       ```rust
       while next_keyframe_time <= effective_time {
           next_keyframe_time += KEYFRAME_INTERVAL;
       }
       ```
       This handles gaps spanning multiple intervals (e.g., a 17s gap should produce one keyframe, not three).
     - `EventType::Output` with `EventData::Text(data)`: call `vt.feed_str(&data)`.
     - `EventType::Resize` with `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`: feed `format!("\x1b[8;{rows};{cols}t")` to the Vt. **Critical**: the xtwinops escape sequence takes rows then cols — the opposite order from `EventData::Resize { cols, rows }`.
     - `EventType::Input` and `EventType::Marker`: skip (no terminal state change).

3. Implement `KeyframeIndex::keyframe_at(time: f64) -> Option<usize>`:

   - Binary search for the last keyframe with `keyframe.time <= time`.
   - Use `partition_point` (same pattern as `TimeMap::event_index_at`).
   - Return the index into the keyframes vec, or `None` if empty or time is before the first keyframe.

4. Implement accessors:

   - `get(index: usize) -> Option<&Keyframe>`
   - `len() -> usize`
   - `is_empty() -> bool`

5. Add `pub mod index;` to `lib.rs`. Re-export: `pub use index::{Keyframe, KeyframeIndex, KEYFRAME_INTERVAL};`

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `Keyframe` struct with `time`, `event_index`, `snapshot` fields
- [ ] `KeyframeIndex` wraps `Vec<Keyframe>` with build/query methods
- [ ] `build()` replays events through avt, captures snapshots at correct effective-time intervals
- [ ] Initial keyframe has `time = 0.0` for non-empty recordings; empty recordings produce empty index
- [ ] Output events feed to Vt, resize events resize Vt via xtwinops (rows,cols order), input/marker skipped
- [ ] Large time gaps produce a single keyframe (not backfilled), and `next_keyframe_time` advances correctly past the gap
- [ ] `keyframe_at()` returns correct index via binary search
- [ ] Module added to `lib.rs` with re-exports
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
notes:
## Codebase context

**Modules you depend on (all complete and tested):**

- `snapshot.rs`: `TerminalSnapshot::capture(vt: &avt::Vt) -> Self` and `create_vt(cols: usize, rows: usize) -> avt::Vt` (resizable, zero scrollback).
- `timemap.rs`: `TimeMap::effective_time(index: usize) -> Option<f64>` for O(1) effective time lookup by event index.
- `parser.rs`: `Recording { header, events, markers }` where `Event { time, event_type, data }` and `EventData::Text(String) | Resize { cols, rows }`.

**Resize parameter order trap**: `EventData::Resize { cols: 120, rows: 40 }` but xtwinops is `\x1b[8;40;120t` (rows;cols). Validated in `snapshot.rs:240`: `vt.feed_str("\x1b[8;40;120t")` produces a 120×40 terminal.

**`feed_str()` return value**: `vt.feed_str()` returns `(Vec<usize>, bool)` — dirty line indices and cursor-moved flag. Ignore the return value; we don't need incremental dirty tracking for index building.

## Verification commands

```bash
cargo build -p speedrun-core
cargo clippy -p speedrun-core -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

title: 1.4.2 — Keyframe index tests
description:
Comprehensive tests for the keyframe index builder and lookup.

## Tasks

1. **Empty recording** (header only, no events): Index should be empty (`len() == 0`, `is_empty() == true`).

2. **Short recording (< 5s effective)**: Use `minimal_v2.cast`. Only the initial keyframe should exist (keyframe 0). Verify its `time == 0.0`, `event_index == 0`, and snapshot is the initial empty terminal.

3. **Multiple keyframe intervals**: Construct a synthetic `Recording` with events spanning >15s of effective time (e.g., output events at 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15s). Build with `TimeMap`. Verify correct number of keyframes, correct `time` values, and correct `event_index` values.

4. **Idle-capped recording**: Load `long_idle.cast` (has `idle_time_limit: 2`, raw events at ~[0, 1, 35, 35.1, 36]). Build TimeMap with the header's idle limit. Effective duration is ~4.1s — less than one keyframe interval. Verify only the initial keyframe exists. This confirms keyframes align to effective time, not raw time.

5. **Smoke test with all test data files**: Build the index from each test data file (`minimal_v2.cast`, `minimal_v3.cast`, `empty.cast`, `long_idle.cast`, `with_markers.cast`, `with_resize.cast`, `alternate_buffer.cast`, `real_session.cast`). Verify each builds without panic and produces a reasonable keyframe count. This is a single test function with a loop — not individual tests per file.

6. **`keyframe_at()` lookup correctness**: Using the multi-interval index from task 3:
   - Exact keyframe time → returns that keyframe's index
   - Time between two keyframes → returns the earlier keyframe's index
   - Time before first keyframe → returns `None`
   - Time at or past last keyframe → returns last keyframe's index
   - Empty index → returns `None`

7. **Insta snapshot test**: For the synthetic multi-interval recording, snapshot the vec of `(time, event_index)` tuples from all keyframes. This locks down the keyframe placement algorithm.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 7 test scenarios pass
- [ ] Tests use existing test data files where possible, synthetic recordings for precise timing control
- [ ] `keyframe_at()` edge cases comprehensively covered
- [ ] At least one insta snapshot test
- [ ] `cargo test -p speedrun-core` all green
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes

title: Epic 2.1 — CLI and Application Bootstrap
description:
Set up the CLI interface, terminal lifecycle management, and application skeleton for the TUI player. This is the entry point and process lifecycle — everything else in the TUI hangs on this.

## Context

Phase 1 (speedrun-core) is complete. The core `Player` API provides `load()`, `load_with()`, `seek()`, `tick()`, and all playback control. This epic wires that API into a running binary with proper terminal management.

### Key design decisions

- **clap derive API** for CLI parsing — consistent with Rust ecosystem conventions.
- **Terminal lifecycle** uses crossterm's raw mode + alternate screen. A panic hook ensures the terminal is always restored.
- **App struct** owns the Player and display state. The event loop is event-driven with deadline (no fixed tick rate).
- **Playback starts immediately** on launch (like `asciinema play`).
- `--start-at` seeks to position after load, then starts playing.

### Dependencies

- `speedrun-core` (path dep) — `Player`, `LoadOptions`, `PlayerError`
- `clap` (derive) — CLI argument parsing
- `crossterm` — raw mode, alternate screen, event polling
- `ratatui` — Terminal type (for the skeleton loop; real rendering is Epic 2.2)

## Scope

- `crates/speedrun/src/main.rs` — rewrite from stub to full entry point
- `crates/speedrun/src/app.rs` — new: App struct + event loop

## Tickets

- 2.1.1 — CLI parsing with clap
- 2.1.2 — Terminal lifecycle and panic hook
- 2.1.3 — App struct and skeleton event loop

title: 2.1.1 — CLI parsing with clap
notes:
## Dependency note

The `speedrun` crate will need `avt` and `rgb` as direct dependencies for the TerminalView widget (Epic 2.2). While this ticket doesn't use them, it's a good opportunity to add them to `crates/speedrun/Cargo.toml` now:

```toml
[dependencies]
speedrun-core = { path = "../speedrun-core" }
ratatui.workspace = true
crossterm.workspace = true
clap.workspace = true
avt.workspace = true
rgb.workspace = true
```

This avoids a blocker for the parallel Epic 2.2 agent.

## Clippy `all = "deny"` note

The workspace has `[workspace.lints.clippy] all = "deny"`. This means ALL clippy lints are denied, including ones like `clippy::needless_return`, `clippy::redundant_field_names`, etc. Write clean idiomatic Rust to avoid any clippy warnings.

## Dead code warnings

Since this ticket creates a placeholder `app.rs` and declares `mod app;`, there will be no usages yet. To avoid dead code warnings from clippy, either:
- Don't add `mod app;` until 2.1.3 (preferred)
- Or add `#[allow(dead_code)]` temporarily

Given the sequential dependency (2.1.2 and 2.1.3 come after), skip creating `app.rs` in this ticket. Let 2.1.3 handle it.

title: 2.1.2 — Terminal lifecycle and panic hook
notes:
## ratatui 0.26 API note

In ratatui 0.26.3, the `Frame` struct has `fn size(&self) -> Rect`, NOT `fn area()`. The `area()` method was introduced in a later version. Use `frame.size()` everywhere.

The placeholder `run()` function should use:
```rust
let area = frame.size();  // NOT frame.area()
```

## Ctrl-C handling

crossterm in raw mode intercepts Ctrl-C. It arrives as a `KeyEvent` with `KeyCode::Char('c')` and `KeyModifiers::CONTROL`. The placeholder `run()` function should handle it:

```rust
if matches!(key.code, KeyCode::Char('q') | KeyCode::Esc)
    || (key.code == KeyCode::Char('c') && key.modifiers.contains(KeyModifiers::CONTROL))
{
    break;
}
```

Without this, Ctrl-C won't work and the user will be stuck in raw mode.

## `player` parameter usage

The `run()` placeholder function accepts `player` and `args` parameters but doesn't use them yet. Prefix with underscore to avoid clippy warnings: `_player: Player, _args: &Args`.

description:
## Summary

## Context

No benchmarks exist. No test recordings larger than ~30s / 115 events. The requirements specify performance targets (<50ms seek, <100ms load typical, <500ms for 10-minute recordings) but there is no infrastructure to verify them. The test suite currently runs in ~0.03s total — all unit tests with small fixtures.

## Scope

- Deterministic recording generator for benchmarks
- Criterion benchmark suite: parse, index, seek at multiple recording sizes
- Performance target verification and documentation
- CPU idle verification (compute_timeout behavior)
- Keyframe count verification

## Tickets

1. 4.2.1 — Benchmark infrastructure + benchmarks
2. 4.2.2 — Performance targets and profiling (depends on 4.2.1)

## Ordering

Strictly sequential: 4.2.2 depends on 4.2.1.

title: 4.2.1 — Benchmark infrastructure + benchmarks
description:
## Summary

Set up criterion benchmarks with a deterministic recording generator. No benchmarks or large test recordings currently exist.

## Context

The largest test recording is `real_session.cast` (115 events, 26KB, ~30s). All others are trivially small (<10 events). For meaningful benchmarks of parsing, index building, and seek operations, we need recordings with thousands of events. These should be generated at benchmark runtime, not committed to the repo.

## Implementation

1. Add dependencies to `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml`:
   ```toml
   [dev-dependencies]
   criterion = "0.5"
   rand = { version = "0.9", features = ["std_rng"] }
   # ... existing dev-deps (insta, tempfile, pretty_assertions)

   [[bench]]
   name = "benchmarks"
   harness = false
   ```
   Also add `criterion` and `rand` to the `[workspace.dependencies]` section in the root `Cargo.toml` if using workspace dependency inheritance.

2. Create the recording generator at `crates/speedrun-core/benches/gen.rs`:
   - Public function: `pub fn generate_recording(duration_secs: u64, seed: u64) -> Vec<u8>`
   - Returns a complete asciicast v2 recording as bytes (JSON header + JSON event lines)
   - Use `rand::rngs::StdRng` with `SeedableRng::seed_from_u64(seed)` for determinism
   - Terminal size: 80x24 (fixed, matches typical recordings)
   - Event rate: ~3 output events per second. Generate timestamps with monotonically increasing values: running clock starting at 0.0, adding `0.2 + rng.gen::<f64>() * 0.5` seconds between events (~1.4-7.1 events per second, averaging ~3)
   - Event data: mix of printable text and ANSI escape sequences. For each event, randomly choose between:
     - Short text line with newline (e.g., `"$ ls -la\r\n"`)
     - SGR color sequence (e.g., `"\x1b[38;5;{n}m{text}\x1b[0m"`)
     - Cursor movement (e.g., `"\x1b[{row};{col}H{text}"`)
   - No input, resize, or marker events — keep the generator simple. Output events are what drive VT processing cost.

3. Create the benchmark entry point at `crates/speedrun-core/benches/benchmarks.rs`:
   ```rust
   mod gen;

   use criterion::{Criterion, criterion_group, criterion_main};
   use std::io::Cursor;
   ```
   Benchmark groups (each group benchmarks 4 recording sizes: 1min, 5min, 10min, 30min):

   - **parse**: Generate recording in group setup (outside `b.iter()`). Inside `b.iter()`: `speedrun_core::parse(Cursor::new(data.clone()))`. The generator call is NOT part of the measured time.
   - **index_build**: Parse the recording once in setup. Inside `b.iter()`: `KeyframeIndex::build(&recording, &time_map, 5.0)`.
   - **seek_worst_case**: Build a full `Player` in setup. Inside `b.iter()`: `player.seek(player.duration())` (seek from implicit start to end).
   - **seek_random**: Build a full `Player` in setup. Generate 10 seek targets from a seeded RNG in setup. Inside `b.iter()`: loop over all 10 targets calling `player.seek(t)`.

4. Add one unit test for the generator (in `benchmarks.rs` or a separate test): `generate_recording(10, 42)` → parse the output → assert no errors and `events.len()` is approximately `10 * 3 = 30` (within ±50% due to randomized event rate). This catches generator bugs that would produce misleading benchmarks.

5. Verify benchmarks run: `cargo bench -p speedrun-core` should complete without errors and print timing results.

## Files touched

- `Cargo.toml` (workspace root) — add criterion and rand to workspace deps
- `crates/speedrun-core/Cargo.toml` — add dev-deps, add [[bench]] target
- `crates/speedrun-core/benches/benchmarks.rs` — benchmark entry point
- `crates/speedrun-core/benches/gen.rs` — recording generator
acceptance_criteria:
- `cargo bench -p speedrun-core` runs and produces timing results for all four benchmark groups (parse, index_build, seek_worst_case, seek_random) at all four recording sizes (1min, 5min, 10min, 30min).
- Generator is deterministic: `generate_recording(10, 42)` produces identical bytes across runs.
- Generated recordings include ANSI escape sequences (SGR colors, cursor movement), not just printable text.
- Generator validation test passes: generated recording parses without errors, event count is approximately duration * 3.
- Benchmarks use release profile (criterion default).
- Generator code is NOT measured in benchmark iterations (recording created in setup, not inside b.iter()).
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes.
- cargo fmt --check passes.

title: 4.2.2 — Performance targets and profiling
description:
## Summary

Run benchmarks, verify performance targets from REQUIREMENTS.md, and document results. This ticket's deliverable is results and documentation, not optimization — if targets aren't met, file follow-up tickets.

## Context

REQUIREMENTS.md specifies: <50ms seek for typical recordings, <100ms load for typical recordings, <500ms load for 10-minute recordings. Memory usage should be proportional and reasonable (~58KB per keyframe for 80x24 terminal). CPU during idle playback should be near zero (event-driven, no spinning).

## Implementation

1. Run benchmarks from f9p.1 in release mode (`cargo bench -p speedrun-core`). Record results.

2. Verify targets against benchmark output. "Typical" recordings means the 1min and 5min benchmarks. All targets apply to `--release` builds:
   - <50ms seek (1min and 5min recordings)
   - <100ms load+index (1min and 5min recordings)
   - <500ms load+index (10-minute recording)

3. Verify CPU idle: add a unit test in `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` tests confirming `time_to_next_event()` returns `Some(duration)` with a non-zero duration when the player is paused and not at the end. This confirms the event loop can sleep rather than busy-loop. **Do not test compute_timeout here** — that is in the TUI crate and its behavior depends on nhw.1. This test only verifies the core engine provides the right sleep hint.

4. Verify keyframe count: add a unit test in `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` tests. Load `real_session.cast`, build index with interval 5.0. The expected keyframe count is `floor(duration / interval) + 1` (one at t=0 plus one every `interval` seconds). Assert the actual count matches. Use `player.duration()` to get the exact duration rather than hardcoding.

5. Record benchmark results in a committed file: `crates/speedrun-core/benches/RESULTS.md`. Include: hardware description (CPU, RAM), benchmark output summary (table of group × size → median time), pass/fail against each target.

6. If any targets are missed, add a TODO comment in `RESULTS.md` noting which target failed and by how much. Follow-up optimization tickets will be created during review. This ticket is done regardless of whether targets are met.

## Files touched

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/player.rs` — new test for time_to_next_event idle behavior
- `crates/speedrun-core/src/index.rs` — new test for keyframe count
- `crates/speedrun-core/benches/RESULTS.md` — new file, benchmark results

## Dependency

Must follow f9p.1 (needs benchmarks to exist).
acceptance_criteria:
- Benchmark results are documented in crates/speedrun-core/benches/RESULTS.md: hardware, benchmark output summary, pass/fail per target.
- Performance targets are either met or RESULTS.md contains TODO notes with specific numbers for each missed target.
- time_to_next_event test passes: returns Some(non-zero duration) when paused with events remaining.
- Keyframe count test passes: index for real_session.cast with interval 5.0 produces floor(duration / 5.0) + 1 keyframes.
- All existing tests pass under cargo test --workspace.
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes.
- cargo fmt --check passes.

title: MP4 export via ffmpeg
description: Add MP4 video export to speedrun using ffmpeg as a subprocess. The existing ScreenRenderer already produces RGBA pixel buffers for each frame; this epic pipes those frames to ffmpeg for H.264 encoding into MP4 containers. Design decisions: ffmpeg subprocess (not native codec library), strictly MP4 output, video-only (no silent audio track), ffmpeg handles RGBA→YUV420p conversion, and ffmpeg handles even-dimension padding via -vf pad filter. Three tickets: (1) core export module in speedrun-export, (2) CLI subcommand, (3) tests.

title: Core MP4 export module
description:
Add crates/speedrun-export/src/mp4.rs with the core export function and tests.

## Dependencies

No new Cargo.toml dependencies required. This uses std::process::Command to spawn ffmpeg as a subprocess — no ffmpeg crate.

## Mp4Options struct

- fps: u32 (default 30)
- scale: u32 (default 1)
- crf: u8 (default 23, range 0-51, lower = better quality)
- export: ExportOptions (shared palette/color config)

Implement Default trait matching these values.

## Mp4Error enum

- Io(std::io::Error) — pipe/file errors
- FfmpegNotFound — ffmpeg not on PATH
- FfmpegFailed { exit_code: Option<i32>, stderr: String } — non-zero exit

Implement Display, Error, and From<std::io::Error>.

## export_mp4() function

Signature: fn export_mp4(player: &mut Player, options: &Mp4Options, output_path: &Path, progress: Option<&dyn Fn(usize, usize)>) -> Result<(), Mp4Error>

Takes output_path (not impl Write) because ffmpeg writes the file directly — MP4 containers require seekable output for the moov atom, so piping to stdout is not viable without fragmented muxing hacks.

### Implementation steps

1. Check ffmpeg availability upfront: spawn Command::new("ffmpeg").arg("-version").output(). If it fails with NotFound, return Mp4Error::FfmpegNotFound.

2. Compute frame dimensions: use ScreenRenderer to get img_w = w * cell_width, img_h = h * cell_height. The -vf pad filter handles even-dimension enforcement, so no Rust-side padding needed.

3. Spawn ffmpeg with stdin/stderr piped:
   ffmpeg -y -f rawvideo -pix_fmt rgba -s {img_w}x{img_h} -r {fps} -i pipe:0 -vf "pad=ceil(iw/2)*2:ceil(ih/2)*2" -c:v libx264 -preset medium -crf {crf} -pix_fmt yuv420p -an {output_path}

   The -y flag is intentional: the CLI layer (fjk.2) handles --force/existence checks before calling export_mp4. The library function always overwrites.

4. **Critical: stderr draining.** Spawn a separate thread to read ffmpeg's stderr to completion BEFORE calling wait(). Without this, if ffmpeg fills its stderr pipe buffer (~64KB on Linux) while we are still writing frames to stdin, both processes deadlock. Pattern:
   - let stderr = child.stderr.take().unwrap();
   - let stderr_thread = std::thread::spawn(move || { let mut buf = String::new(); BufReader::new(stderr).read_to_string(&mut buf); buf });
   - ... write all frames to child.stdin ...
   - drop(child.stdin.take()); // close stdin to signal EOF
   - let stderr_output = stderr_thread.join().unwrap();
   - let status = child.wait()?;

5. Frame iteration (same pattern as gif.rs:183-205):
   for frame_idx in 0..total_frames {
       let time = frame_idx as f64 / fps as f64;
       player.seek(time);
       let img = renderer.render_frame(player.screen(), &player.cursor(), w, h);
       stdin.write_all(img.as_raw())?;  // raw RGBA bytes
       if let Some(cb) = progress { cb(frame_idx + 1, total_frames); }
   }

6. After closing stdin and joining the stderr thread, check exit status. If non-zero, return Mp4Error::FfmpegFailed with the captured stderr.

7. Emit a warning to stderr for long recordings (>300s), matching GIF behavior.

### Register in lib.rs

Add pub mod mp4; to crates/speedrun-export/src/lib.rs.

## Tests

All tests live in a #[cfg(test)] mod tests block at the bottom of mp4.rs, following the pattern in gif.rs.

### Always-run tests (no ffmpeg needed)

- test_mp4_options_default: Mp4Options::default() returns fps=30, scale=1, crf=23
- test_mp4_error_display: Mp4Error::FfmpegNotFound and Mp4Error::FfmpegFailed display sensible messages
- test_mp4_ffmpeg_not_found: Set child process PATH to "/nonexistent" via Command::new("ffmpeg").env("PATH", "/nonexistent") — this is per-process and thread-safe unlike std::env::set_var. Verify export returns Mp4Error::FfmpegNotFound. (Note: factor out the ffmpeg detection into a helper function like fn check_ffmpeg() -> Result<(), Mp4Error> to make this testable without running the full export.)

### ffmpeg-gated tests (marked #[ignore])

These run with cargo test -- --include-ignored when ffmpeg (and ffprobe) are available.

- test_mp4_export_produces_valid_file: Export minimal recording to a tempfile::NamedTempFile path. Verify output file exists and the first bytes contain the ftyp MP4 box (bytes 4..8 == b"ftyp").
- test_mp4_frame_count: Export a 2-second recording at 10 fps. Run ffprobe -v error -count_frames -select_streams v:0 -show_entries stream=nb_read_frames on the output. Verify frame count is 20 +/-1.
- test_mp4_dimensions_are_even: Export a recording with odd terminal dimensions (e.g. 81 cols). Run ffprobe to check width and height are both even.
- test_mp4_progress_callback: Export with a callback, verify it is called total_frames times and the last call has current == total.

Use tempfile::NamedTempFile for all test output files (tempfile is already a dev-dependency in the workspace).

## Acceptance criteria

- export_mp4() produces a valid MP4 file when ffmpeg is available
- Returns Mp4Error::FfmpegNotFound gracefully when ffmpeg is not installed
- Returns Mp4Error::FfmpegFailed with captured stderr on encoding errors
- No deadlock: stderr is drained in a separate thread while frames are written to stdin
- Progress callback is invoked for each frame
- Even-dimension padding is handled by the ffmpeg -vf pad filter
- No new entries in Cargo.toml (pure subprocess approach)
- All non-ignored tests pass without ffmpeg
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes
- cargo test --workspace passes
notes:
## Additional test (added during review)

Add one more ffmpeg-gated (#[ignore]) test:

- test_mp4_empty_recording: Export a recording with no events (header only, 0 duration). Verify it does not error or hang, and produces a valid output file. This mirrors test_gif_single_frame_empty_recording in gif.rs.

title: MP4 CLI subcommand
description:
Add the 'speedrun export mp4' CLI subcommand in crates/speedrun/src/main.rs.

## Changes to ExportFormat enum (main.rs)

Add Mp4 variant with fields:
- file: PathBuf (positional)
- output: PathBuf (--output, -o, required)
- fps: u32 (--fps, default 30)
- scale: u32 (--scale, default 1)
- crf: u8 (--crf, default 23)
- force: bool (--force)

## New run_export_mp4() function

**Important: this does NOT follow the run_export_gif() pattern exactly.** GIF export uses open_output() to create a File writer, then passes it to export_gif(). MP4 export takes an output_path: &Path because ffmpeg writes the file directly. The differences:

1. Do NOT call open_output(). Instead, check for existing file manually:
   if output.exists() && !force {
       eprintln!("Output file already exists: {}. Use --force to overwrite.", output.display());
       std::process::exit(1);
   }

2. Call export_mp4(&mut player, &opts, &output, Some(&progress_callback))
   — pass the path, not a writer.

3. Otherwise follows the same structure: load_player(), print warnings, set up progress callback (every 100 frames + final frame), print "Wrote {path}" on success.

Feature-gated behind #[cfg(feature = "export")] like SVG and GIF.

## Wire into main()

Add ExportFormat::Mp4 { .. } arm to the match format { ... } dispatch block in main().
Include the #[cfg(not(feature = "export"))] fallback with the standard error message.

## Acceptance criteria

- 'speedrun export mp4 recording.cast -o output.mp4' works end-to-end
- --fps, --scale, --crf, --force flags are accepted and passed through
- --force overwrites existing output file
- Without --force, exits with error if output file exists
- Does NOT use open_output() — manually checks Path::exists() for the --force gate
- Progress is printed to stderr (matching GIF pattern: every 100 frames + final)
- Feature gate works: disabled export feature prints helpful error
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes
- cargo test --workspace passes

title: Tests for MP4 export
description:
Add tests for the MP4 export module in crates/speedrun-export/src/mp4.rs.

**Always-run tests (no ffmpeg needed):**
- Mp4Options::default() returns expected values (fps=30, scale=1, crf=23)
- Mp4Error display messages are sensible strings

**ffmpeg-gated tests (marked #[ignore]):**
These run with 'cargo test -- --include-ignored' when ffmpeg is available.

- test_mp4_export_produces_valid_file: Export minimal recording, verify output file exists and starts with ftyp MP4 box magic bytes
- test_mp4_frame_count: Verify ffprobe reports expected frame count (fps * duration, +/-1)
- test_mp4_dimensions_are_even: Verify ffprobe reports even width and height (padding applied)

**Acceptance criteria:**

title: Epic 5.2 — Marker authoring core
description: Core library support for creating markers and converting them to asciicast format. No file I/O (stays WASM-compatible). Three tickets: parser fix for out-of-order events (prerequisite for append-based authoring), reverse time mapping (effective→raw), and marker serialization + Player::add_marker(). Epic 5.1 and 5.2 can run in parallel.

title: 5.2.1 — Parser: accept out-of-order events
description:
## Summary

The v2 parser currently skips events with timestamps earlier than the preceding event (`parser.rs:483-496`), producing a warning and discarding the event. Marker authoring appends marker events to the end of the `.cast` file with raw times that may be earlier than the last event. Without this fix, appended markers are **silently dropped on reload**.

This is a prerequisite for all marker authoring work.

## Files to Modify

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/parser.rs`:
  1. **Remove the v2 monotonicity skip** (lines ~483-496). Change from `continue` to accepting the event. Still emit a warning for diagnostics, but do NOT skip the event. Keep updating `prev_time` only for in-order events (so the warning accurately reports which events are out of order).
  2. **Sort events after parsing** (after the `for line_result in lines` loop, before building the `Recording`). Use `events.sort_by(|a, b| a.time.partial_cmp(&b.time).unwrap_or(std::cmp::Ordering::Equal))` — this is a stable sort, preserving file order for same-timestamp events.
  3. **Re-derive markers from sorted events.** Currently markers are collected during the parse loop (line ~571-574) in parallel with events. After sorting events, the markers vec is out of sync. Replace the post-loop markers vec by re-deriving it from the sorted events:
     ```rust
     let markers: Vec<Marker> = events.iter()
         .filter(|e| e.event_type == EventType::Marker)
         .map(|e| Marker {
             time: e.time,
             label: match &e.data {
                 EventData::Text(s) => s.clone(),
                 _ => String::new(),
             },
         })
         .collect();
     ```
     Remove the inline `markers.push()` from the parse loop, or keep it and just replace the vec after sorting.

## Design Notes

- **V3 is unaffected.** V3 uses relative timestamps that are accumulated into absolute times during parsing (lines ~470-481). The absolute times are monotonically increasing by construction. The sort is a no-op for v3 files.
- **Backward compatibility.** For properly ordered files, stable sort preserves the original order. All existing parse results are identical.
- `TimeMap::build()` receives raw times from the sorted events vec, so no changes needed there.

## Tests

All tests in `crates/speedrun-core/src/parser.rs` under `mod tests`. Use inline recordings (byte strings), following the existing test pattern:

1. **`test_out_of_order_marker_accepted`** — V2 file with output events at t=1.0, 3.0, 8.0 and a marker `[5.0, "m", "mid"]` appended after the t=8.0 event. Assert: `recording.events` has 4 events sorted by time. `recording.markers` has 1 marker at time 5.0. Marker appears between t=3.0 and t=8.0 events.

2. **`test_out_of_order_multiple_events_sorted`** — V2 file with events at t=5.0, 1.0, 3.0 (all out of order). Assert: `recording.events` sorted as [1.0, 3.0, 5.0].

3. **`test_out_of_order_emits_warning`** — Same file as test 1. Assert: `recording.warnings` contains at least one warning mentioning "non-decreasing" or similar.

4. **`test_stable_sort_preserves_file_order`** — V2 file with two output events at the same timestamp: `[1.0, "o", "first"]`, `[1.0, "o", "second"]`. Assert: after parsing, `events[0].data` is "first" and `events[1].data` is "second" (file order preserved).

5. **`test_v3_unaffected_by_sort`** — V3 file with relative timestamps [0.5, 0.7, 0.8]. Assert: events have correct accumulated absolute times [0.5, 1.2, 2.0], no warnings about ordering.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A `.cast` file with an appended out-of-order marker event parses successfully.
- [ ] The marker appears in `recording.markers` at the correct sorted position.

title: 5.2.2 — Reverse time mapping (effective → raw)
description:
## Summary

Add the ability to convert effective times back to raw times in `TimeMap`, using the "uncapped delta" strategy. This is needed for marker authoring: the user sees effective time, but the `.cast` file stores raw time.

## Files to Modify

- `crates/speedrun-core/src/timemap.rs`:
  - Add `raw_times: Vec<f64>` field to the `TimeMap` struct.
  - In `build()`: store `raw_times: raw_times.to_vec()` (the function already receives `raw_times: &[f64]`, so no signature change needed).
  - Add method:
    ```rust
    /// Convert an effective time back to a raw time using the "uncapped delta" strategy.
    ///
    /// Returns `None` if the effective time is before the first event or the map is empty.
    /// For effective times at or past the last event, returns the last raw time.
    pub fn raw_time(&self, effective: f64) -> Option<f64> {
        if self.effective_times.is_empty() {
            return None;
        }
        // Binary search for the last event at or before `effective`
        let count = self.effective_times.partition_point(|&t| t <= effective);
        if count == 0 {
            return None; // before first event
        }
        let idx = count - 1;
        // Uncapped delta: raw = prev_raw + (effective - prev_eff)
        let delta = effective - self.effective_times[idx];
        Some(self.raw_times[idx] + delta)
    }
    ```

## Algorithm — "uncapped delta" (NOT linear interpolation)

Binary search `effective_times` for the last event at or before the target. Compute:
```
raw = prev_raw + (effective - prev_eff)
```

**Why not linear interpolation?** Linear interpolation across idle-capped gaps produces raw times deep inside the gap, which causes markers to shift position and recording duration to increase disproportionately on reload. The uncapped-delta approach preserves the effective-time position because the delta from the preceding event is always ≤ the idle limit (it came from an already-capped effective gap), so it will never be re-capped on reload.

**Example:**
- Raw events: `[0.0, 1.0, 100.0]`, idle_limit=2.0
- Effective: `[0.0, 1.0, 3.0]`
- User at effective=2.0 → `raw = 1.0 + (2.0 - 1.0) = 2.0`
- On reload: gap 1.0→2.0 is 1.0s (uncapped), gap 2.0→100.0 is 98.0s (capped to 2.0)
- Marker at effective=2.0 ✓ (correct position preserved)

## Edge Cases

- Empty map → `None`.
- Before first event (effective < first_eff) → `None`.
- At exact event time → `delta == 0`, returns exact raw time for that event. No f64-equality special case needed.
- At/past last event → `idx == last`, `delta >= 0`, returns `last_raw + delta`. When `effective == last_eff`, returns `last_raw` exactly.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `raw_time(eff)` returns `Some(raw)` for any effective time within the recording range.
- [ ] `raw_time()` returns `None` for effective times before the first event.
- [ ] `raw_time()` returns `None` for empty time map.
- [ ] For recordings with no idle limit, `raw_time(t) == Some(t)` for all event times (raw == effective).
- [ ] For recordings with idle limit, round-trip holds: for every event index `i`, `raw_time(effective_time(i).unwrap())` returns `Some(original_raw_time)`.
- [ ] Within an idle-capped gap: `raw_time(prev_eff + delta)` returns `prev_raw + delta` (NOT linear interpolation across the full raw gap).
- [ ] `TimeMap` memory increase is exactly `size_of::<f64>() * num_events`.
- [ ] All existing `TimeMap` tests still pass (no signature changes).
- [ ] New tests: no-limit round-trip, idle-limited round-trip, uncapped-delta in gap, before-first, at-last, empty map, at exact event boundaries.
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace` passes.
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` passes.
